34. **Time and date are marked with `<time>`.** The `datetime` attribute provides a machine-readable format. *Reason:* Allows browsers and bots to parse events, schedules, and publication dates. *Example:* `<time datetime="2026-07-05">July 5, 2026</time>`. *Guidance:* Use the ISO 8601 format for the `datetime` attribute.

35. **Code, preformatted text, and keyboard input use the correct elements.** `<code>` for inline code, `<pre>` for blocks of code, `<kbd>` for keyboard input. *Reason:* It conveys the semantics of technical content. *Example:* Use `<pre><code>...</code></pre>` for code blocks. *Guidance:* Use syntax highlighting via `<span>` within the `<code>` block if needed.

36. **Emphasis is semantic, not visual.** Use `<em>` for stress emphasis, `<strong>` for strong importance. Do not use them solely to italicize or bolden text. *Reason:* Screen readers change their intonation for `<em>` and `<strong>`. *Example:* “You *must* save your work” uses `<em>` on “must”. *Guidance:* Use CSS for purely visual styling of text.

37. **The `<iframe>` must have a `title` attribute.** The title should describe the embedded content. *Reason:* Screen reader users can understand what the iframe contains without navigating into it. *Example:* `<iframe title="Zenuxs product demo video" src="...">`. *Guidance:* Never use an iframe without a title.

38. **No auto-playing audio or video without user interaction.** Media must respect the user’s control. *Reason:* Prevents startling and disorienting experiences. *Guidance:* Use the `autoplay` attribute only when muted or when the user has explicitly opted in.

39. **Use the `<picture>` element for responsive images.** It allows serving different images based on media queries. *Reason:* Optimizes performance by loading appropriately sized images. *Example:* A large hero image for desktop and a smaller one for mobile. *Guidance:* Always provide a fallback `<img>`.

40. **SVG icons are inline or used with `<img>` for static, decorative purposes.** If an icon is interactive, it should be inline with appropriate `aria-label` or `<title>`. *Reason:* Inline SVGs are part of the DOM and can be styled and animated. *Example:* An interactive hamburger menu icon is an inline SVG with a `<title>Menu</title>`. *Guidance:* Use an SVG sprite or a dedicated icon component.

41. **Every document has a single `<main>` element.** It contains the primary, unique content of the page. *Reason:* It is the designated landmark for the page’s core content. *Example:* A documentation page’s `<main>` contains the article; the sidebar and header are outside it. *Guidance:* Do not nest other landmark elements (like `<nav>`) inside `<main>` unless they are part of the main content.

42. **Navigation is marked with `<nav>`.** The primary navigation is a `<nav>` with an `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` if there are multiple. *Reason:* Provides quick access for screen reader users. *Example:* `<nav aria-label="Main">`. *Guidance:* Do not use `<nav>` for every list of links; only for major navigation blocks.

43. **The `<aside>` element is for tangentially related content.** Like a sidebar, callout, or advertisement. *Reason:* Screen readers can differentiate supplementary content. *Example:* A sidebar with related articles is an `<aside>`. *Guidance:* If the content in the `<aside>` is essential, it might not be truly supplementary.

44. **Use the `<dialog>` element for modal windows.** It provides built-in focus trapping and backdrop semantics with the `showModal()` method. *Reason:* It is the native, accessible way to create modals. *Example:* A confirmation dialog. *Guidance:* Always use `showModal()` for modals; `show()` for non-modal dialogs.

45. **Forms have a submit button.** Use `<button type="submit">`. Avoid using a `<div>` or an `<a>` to submit a form. *Reason:* It works with the Enter key, is accessible by default, and is semantically correct. *Guidance:* A form should always be submittable without JavaScript.

46. **Use the `<template>` element for client-side HTML fragments that are not rendered on page load.** *Reason:* It’s inert content that can be cloned and activated by JavaScript. *Example:* A skeleton for a new list item that gets added dynamically. *Guidance:* Never put visible content directly inside a `<template>`.

47. **The `<noscript>` tag is used to provide a fallback when JavaScript is disabled.** Even in a JS-heavy app, a simple message should be present. *Reason:* Graceful degradation. *Example:* “Zenuxs requires JavaScript to run. Please enable it in your browser settings.” *Guidance:* It should not be an afterthought.

48. **Use `rel` attributes on links appropriately.** `rel="noopener"` for `target="_blank"`, `rel="nofollow"` for untrusted user content, `rel="preconnect"` for early connections. *Reason:* Security, performance, and SEO. *Guidance:* Automate the addition of `rel="noopener noreferrer"` for external links.

49. **Document-level metadata is complete.** The `<head>` contains charset, viewport, title, description, favicon, and social media meta tags (Open Graph, Twitter Cards) for shareable pages. *Reason:* It’s how the web understands your page. *Guidance:* Use a meta tag generator or a standard template.

50. **The HTML is valid.** It passes the W3C Nu HTML Checker without errors. Warnings are addressed. *Reason:* Validity is the baseline of correctness; invalid HTML can cause unpredictable rendering and accessibility bugs. *Guidance:* Integrate HTML validation into the CI pipeline.

## Semantic Philosophy

Semantic HTML is the practice of choosing the element that best describes the meaning and role of the content it contains. It is the primary way we communicate intent to browsers, search engines, and assistive technologies.

**Sectioning elements (`<header>`, `<footer>`, `<main>`, `<nav>`, `<aside>`, `<section>`, `<article>`)** divide the page into logical regions. Each region has a specific purpose: the `<main>` holds the core content, the `<nav>` holds navigation links, the `<aside>` holds supplementary information. Using these elements correctly creates a machine-readable table of contents, known as the document outline. Screen reader users can jump directly to the `<main>` content, bypassing repeated navigation, a fundamental accessibility feature.

**Headings (`<h1>`–`<h6>`)** define the structure of the content. They are not size tags; they are hierarchy tags. An `<h1>` is the main title of the page. An `<h2>` is a major section heading. An `<h3>` is a sub-section, and so on. This hierarchical nesting is critical for screen reader navigation and for SEO.

**Lists (`<ul>`, `<ol>`, `<dl>`)** group related items. Use `<ul>` for unordered collections, `<ol>` for ordered steps or rankings, and `<dl>` for term-description pairs. Never use a series of `<div>` elements when a list is semantically correct. Lists allow screen readers to announce the number of items and allow quick navigation.

**Buttons (`<button>`)** trigger actions. **Links (`<a>`)** navigate. The distinction is absolute. A button submits forms, opens modals, toggles state. A link goes to a new page or a new location on the same page. Using the wrong element breaks user expectations and requires extensive JavaScript to mimic basic functionality. This is a cardinal sin in Zenuxs HTML.

**Forms** collect data. Every form control (`<input>`, `<select>`, `<textarea>`) must have a programmatically associated label. Form validation is communicated semantically. The form itself has a clear submission mechanism. A well-built form is usable with the keyboard alone.

**Dialogs (`<dialog>`)** are modal or non-modal interactive overlays. The native `<dialog>` element provides a built-in backdrop, focus trapping, and accessible semantics when used with `showModal()`. Custom modal implementations are forbidden unless the native element is provably insufficient for an edge case.

**Landmarks** are a subset of semantic elements that define the page’s major areas. Using them is not optional; it is a requirement for a WCAG-compliant experience. Every page must have at least a `<header>`, `<main>`, and `<footer>` landmark.

Why semantics matter: they affect accessibility by giving assistive technologies the hooks to navigate and understand the page. They affect SEO by giving search engines a clear signal of content structure and importance. They affect maintainability by making the code self-documenting—a `<nav>` is obviously navigation, while a `<div class="nav">` is a puzzle. They affect developer experience because native elements provide free functionality (like the Enter key submitting a form) that custom components must manually re-implement. In short, semantics are an engineering multiplier: they do more with less code.

## Document Architecture

A Zenuxs HTML document is not a blank canvas; it is a blueprint of a specific information architecture. The document structure must be as intentional and robust as any backend service.

**Overall document structure** follows a consistent pattern: `<!DOCTYPE html>` at the top, `<html lang="en">`, `<head>` with essential metadata, and `<body>` containing the visual and interactive surface. The `<head>` is a machine-readable instruction set; the `<body>` is the human-readable and assistive-technology-readable content. This separation is maintained rigorously.

**Reading order** is the sequence in which content appears in the DOM. It must match the logical flow of the content, independent of visual layout. A screen reader reads the DOM top-to-bottom; a keyboard user tabs through it. Therefore, the most important content—the “skip to main content” link, the header, the main content, the footer—appears in that order. Modals and other overlays are appended near the end of the `<body>` to keep their focus management contained, but visually they appear over everything.

**Content hierarchy** starts with the page’s primary `<h1>`. This is followed by a series of `<section>` or `<article>` elements, each introduced by a heading of the appropriate level. The `<h1>` is the title of the entire page; an `<h2>` is the title of a major section; an `<h3>` is a sub-section within that. No heading level is skipped. This creates a predictable, parseable tree.

**Logical grouping** is achieved through semantic containers. Related form controls are grouped in a `<fieldset>`. A blog post and its comments are an `<article>` containing nested `<article>`s. Navigation links are grouped in a `<nav>` and often in a `<ul>` for listing. Every visual grouping of content should have a corresponding semantic grouping in the HTML.

**Content ownership** is clear: a component owns its root element. A Card component renders an `<article>`; a Button component renders a `<button>`. No external code should rely on the internal DOM structure of a component. This encapsulation allows the HTML of a component to be refactored without breaking the rest of the application.

**Component boundaries** are defined by semantic and data attributes. A parent component passes data to a child via attributes; the child renders its own semantic structure. The parent never injects raw HTML into a child’s internals, preserving encapsulation.

**How documents should scale:** a large, complex page is broken into smaller, semantically distinct chunks. Each chunk is a self-contained section with its own heading and structure. This makes the page navigable by landmarks and headings, and allows developers to reason about one section at a time. A monolithic page of thousands of lines is a failure of document architecture.

**HTML supports long-term maintainability** by being a stable, framework-agnostic foundation. React, Vue, and Svelte may come and go, but semantic HTML is a web standard that has been stable for decades. By keeping the HTML as clean and standard as possible, we ensure that the codebase can survive framework migrations with minimal rewriting. The HTML is the source of truth; JavaScript frameworks are a rendering detail.

## Information Structure

Before any CSS or JavaScript touches the page, the HTML alone must communicate a clear hierarchy of information.

**Primary Content** is the main reason the page exists. It is contained within the `<main>` element and is the first `<h1>` or the most prominent content. For a documentation page, it’s the article text. For the editor, it’s the code editor and its associated toolbars. The primary content must be the most semantically prominent.

**Secondary Content** supports the primary content: a table of contents, a sidebar with related links, a search bar. It is placed in `<aside>`, `<nav>`, or other supporting landmarks. It is visually and semantically subordinate to the primary content.

**Supporting Content** is additional, often repeated, information: a footer with legal links, a cookie banner, a promotional banner. It lives outside the `<main>` and is often the last thing in the DOM order, even if it’s visually fixed.

**Navigation** includes global navigation, local navigation (within a page), and breadcrumb trails. It is always marked with `<nav>`. Multiple `<nav>` elements are distinguished with `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`. The primary navigation is typically the first `<nav>` in the DOM.

**Actions** are interactive elements that allow the user to perform tasks. They are buttons, links, or form controls. They must be clearly labeled and logically grouped near the content they affect.

**Status and Feedback** are dynamic messages like toasts, progress bars, and error summaries. They are placed in a dedicated `<section aria-live="polite">` or similar live region, ensuring they are announced to screen readers without disrupting the user’s current task.

**Metadata** includes the page `<title>`, `<meta>` tags, and structured data (JSON-LD). It is invisible to the user but critical for machines.

The HTML should communicate this hierarchy even when stripped of all styles. A raw, unstyled Zenuxs page should look like a well-formatted document, with clear headings, paragraphs, and sections. This is the acid test of our information architecture.

## Accessibility First HTML

Accessibility is not a checklist appended to the HTML; it is the HTML’s fundamental job. The web is an accessible medium by default; it is our duty not to break it.

**Screen readers** parse the DOM as a linear stream of text and semantics. They rely on landmarks for navigation, headings for structure, and ARIA for interactive widgets. Our HTML must provide a rich, accurate semantic description of the page. A well-structured document with proper headings and landmarks is inherently accessible before any ARIA is added.

**Keyboard navigation** is ensured by using native interactive elements. A `<button>` receives focus and responds to Enter and Space. A `<a>` responds to Enter. Custom interactive elements built from non-semantic elements must be avoided. When a custom widget is absolutely necessary, its keyboard interaction model must exactly mirror a native counterpart, and it must have the correct ARIA role.

**Landmarks** are the signposts of the page. A screen reader user can press a key to jump between them. We must provide a `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, `<footer>`, and optionally `<aside>` and `<form>` landmarks. Every piece of content should reside within a landmark.

**Labels** for form controls and interactive elements are mandatory. An input without a label is an inaccessible mystery. Use the `<label>` element with a `for` attribute, or wrap the input. For icon buttons, use `aria-label`.

**Headings** create a navigable outline. A screen reader user can pull up a list of all headings and jump directly to the section they need. The hierarchy must be logical and continuous. No missing levels.

**Relationships** between elements are expressed through DOM order, semantic tags, and ARIA properties like `aria-labelledby`, `aria-describedby`, and `aria-owns`. For example, a tab panel is related to its tab via `aria-labelledby`.

**Reading order** is determined by the DOM. We must ensure that the DOM order matches the logical reading order, regardless of CSS layout. Content that is visually at the top but at the bottom of the DOM will be encountered last by a screen reader, causing confusion.

**ARIA philosophy:** ARIA exists to fill gaps where native HTML semantics are insufficient. It should be used sparingly and precisely. A page heavy with ARIA is often a sign of poor semantic HTML underneath. Always start with the native element; only add ARIA when you must, and always test with a screen reader.

## SEO-Friendly HTML

Search engines are the most important robots that parse our HTML. Writing semantic, well-structured HTML is the most effective and enduring SEO strategy.

**Semantic hierarchy** tells search engines what the page is about. The `<title>` and `<h1>` are the strongest signals. Subheadings provide context and structure. Using `<article>` for self-contained content helps search engines understand what can be extracted and displayed in rich results.

**Metadata philosophy:** The `<title>` and `<meta name="description">` are the page’s handshake with the search engine. They must be unique for every page, concise, and compelling. The description should contain the key search terms naturally.

**Heading hierarchy** must be logical. A search engine uses headings to build a topical map of the page. A skipped heading level or a missing `<h1>` is a missed opportunity and a sign of poor content structure.

**Content structure** should be text-heavy and meaningful. The most important content should be in plain HTML text, not buried in images or JavaScript. Search engines are getting better at rendering JavaScript, but plain HTML is still the fastest and most reliable path to indexing.

**Machine readability** is enhanced by structured data (JSON-LD). For Zenuxs, this applies to the website, documentation, and blog. Adding `Article`, `BreadcrumbList`, `Organization`, and `SoftwareApplication` schemas helps search engines understand the context and display rich results.

**Meaningful markup:** Use `<strong>` and `<em>` to emphasize key terms. Use `<blockquote>` for quotes. Use `<code>` for code. These semantic tags add nuance that search engines can interpret. The more precisely we describe our content, the better it can be matched to relevant queries.

## Component Structure

HTML is the output of our component rendering layer, whether that’s JSX, Handlebars, or a Web Component. The component’s HTML output must adhere to the same semantic standards as any static page.

**How HTML supports reusable components:** A component encapsulates a specific piece of UI and its semantics. A `<zenuxs-modal>` web component renders a `<dialog>`; a `<zenuxs-card>` renders an `<article>`. The component’s public API (attributes, slots) maps to its semantic structure. This allows the component to be reused while maintaining a consistent, predictable DOM.

**How components remain understandable:** The generated HTML must be as clean as if it were hand-written. Avoid spurious `<div>` wrappers generated by the framework. Use descriptive class names that follow a BEM-like convention, tying back to the component’s purpose. The component’s internal DOM should tell its own story.

**How components remain maintainable:** By isolating styles and behavior within a well-defined HTML structure, components can be developed and tested in isolation. The HTML is the interface between the component’s logic and the outside world (other components, CSS, JavaScript). Changes to the internal HTML should be safe as long as the semantic contract (the landmark, the interactive elements) remains the same.

**How components remain predictable:** A component always renders the same semantic root element for the same logical state. A dropdown component renders a `<button>` and a `<ul>`. It never renders a `<div>` instead of a button, because that would break the semantic contract. This predictability extends to the component’s attributes: `aria-expanded` on the button, `aria-labelledby` linking the list to the button.

## Content Hierarchy

The document’s headings form a strict outline. This outline is the most important structural layer after landmarks.

**Headings:** The page `<h1>` is its title. Only one per page, and it must describe the page’s unique content. The `<h2>`s are the main sections. If a `<section>` or `<article>` is used, it should start with a heading of the correct rank. The HTML5 outline algorithm was never adopted by browsers, so we rely on explicit heading levels. This is the recommended practice.

**Paragraphs:** Use `<p>` for blocks of prose. Do not use `<br>` for spacing between paragraphs; that’s what `<p>` and CSS margins are for. A `<p>` should contain a complete thought.

**Lists:** Use `<ul>` for unordered, `<ol>` for ordered. A list of features is a `<ul>`. A step-by-step guide is an `<ol>`. A navigation menu is a list of links, wrapped in a `<nav>` and often a `<ul>`. The combination of `<nav>` and `<ul>` tells the screen reader both that it’s navigation and how many items there are.

**Tables:** Use only for multi-dimensional data. Include `<caption>` to describe the table’s purpose. Use `<thead>` and `<tbody>`. Mark header cells with `<th scope="col">` or `scope="row"`. Complex tables use `headers` and `id` attributes. This is critical for screen reader users to understand data relationships.

**Media:** Images, videos, and SVGs must have text alternatives. `<img alt="...">` is mandatory. A complex chart should have a longer description nearby, linked with `aria-describedby`. A purely decorative image uses `alt=""` so it is ignored.

**Interactive elements:** As stated, links and buttons are used for their correct purposes. A group of actions is often a `<menu>` (if supported) or a list of buttons.

**Supporting information:** Footnotes, references, and asides use `<footer>` or `<aside>` as appropriate. A footnote is an `<aside>` inside the `<article>`, linked with an anchor.

## Naming Philosophy

The `id` and `class` attributes are the primary hooks for CSS and JavaScript. Their naming must be systematic and meaningful.

**Meaningful IDs:** An `id` should be unique on the page and describe the element’s purpose, not its appearance. `#main-navigation`, `#search-input`, `#user-menu`. Use IDs for landmarks and for elements that are targets for JavaScript or deep linking. Avoid using IDs for styling due to their high specificity.

**Meaningful classes:** Classes describe the component and its variant. Use a consistent methodology like BEM (Block Element Modifier). `.card`, `.card__title`, `.card--featured`. This makes the CSS highly scoped and self-documenting. Never use classes that describe the visual style (`.red-text`, `.margin-top-20`). These are brittle and uninformative.

**Readable attributes:** Data attributes follow a similar pattern: `data-state`, `data-index`, `data-user-id`. They are namespaced and purpose-driven.

**Consistent terminology:** Use the same words in HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. If a component is called “disclosure” in the design system, its class is `.disclosure` and its JavaScript hook is `data-disclosure`. This eliminates mental translation.

**Scalable naming:** The naming system must work for a platform with hundreds of components. BEM and namespaced data attributes provide this. They prevent collisions and make it obvious which component owns which styles and behavior.

## HTML Maintainability

HTML is read far more often than it is written. It is a living document that will be edited by many hands over many years.

**Readable documents:** Use consistent indentation, logical grouping, and comments to explain non-obvious structures. The HTML source is not a build artifact; it’s a human interface during development. Formatting is enforced by an auto-formatter.

**Predictable structure:** Similar components use the same base HTML structure. A developer who knows how a `<zenuxs-card>` is built can guess the structure of a `<zenuxs-modal>` because they share a common semantic language.

**Minimal nesting:** Deeply nested DOM trees are a code smell. Each extra wrapper is an element that must be loaded, rendered, and maintained. Use CSS Grid and Flexbox to flatten layouts. The DOM should be a shallow, broad tree, not a deep, narrow one.

**Logical grouping:** Sections, articles, and lists group related content. This not only aids accessibility but makes the code more scannable. A clear section break in the code reflects a content break in the page.

**Long-term editing:** A developer fixing a bug in five years should be able to open an HTML file and quickly find the relevant section based on its semantic markup and comments. They should not have to trace through dozens of nested `<div>`s with auto-generated class names.

**Future AI understanding:** AI agents, whether for code generation or assistance, parse the project’s HTML. Clean, semantic, well-named markup allows the AI to build an accurate model of the page structure, leading to better suggestions and fewer hallucinations.

**Future contributor understanding:** The HTML is the most stable and universal part of a front-end stack. New contributors may come from different framework backgrounds, but they all understand HTML. A clean HTML foundation lowers the barrier to contribution.

## Progressive Enhancement

Zenuxs HTML is built on the principle of progressive enhancement: start with a solid, semantic, functional base layer (HTML), then add styling (CSS), then add interactivity (JavaScript). This ensures the core content and functionality are accessible to all, regardless of device, network speed, or browser capability.

**HTML first:** The page content and basic interactions (links, forms) must work without JavaScript. A form that submits to a server and returns a response is the baseline. JavaScript enhances it with client-side validation and an inline response, but if JavaScript fails, the form still works.

**Content first:** The most important part of any page is its content. That content must be available in the initial HTML payload, not loaded asynchronously by JavaScript. Critical content is server-rendered. Progressive enhancement ensures the content is indexable by search engines and readable by low-end devices.

**Meaning first:** The semantic structure is the core of the document. CSS and JavaScript enhance its presentation and behavior, but they do not define its meaning. The meaning is stable.

**Behavior second:** JavaScript is an enhancement. It adds rich interactions (like drag-and-drop, real-time updates) that are impossible with HTML alone. But the core tasks (reading, navigating, submitting) remain functional without it.

**Enhancement third:** Advanced features, like AI-powered code completion, are the final layer. They depend on JavaScript and often on network connectivity. The page is fully usable without them, but they provide a powerful upgrade for users whose environment supports them.

Why robust HTML survives technology changes: a form built with semantic HTML and server-side handling will work in any browser, past or future. A form built entirely with a React state management library and `fetch` may break in a decade when that library is abandoned. The HTML layer is our most durable asset.

## HTML Performance

HTML itself is lightweight, but the structure of the DOM has a direct impact on rendering performance.

**Efficient markup:** A shallow, well-structured DOM tree is faster to parse and render. Every unnecessary element adds to the browser’s layout and paint cost. Deeply nested wrappers are a performance anti-pattern.

**Avoid unnecessary DOM depth:** A common cause is over-nesting in component frameworks. Be disciplined: a component should render as few wrapper elements as possible, preferably a single semantic root. Use React Fragments or similar to avoid extra nodes.

**Avoid redundant elements:** An empty `<div>` just for a visual effect, or a `<span>` that serves no semantic or styling purpose, is pure overhead. CSS pseudo-elements (`::before`, `::after`) can often replace empty decoration elements.

**Efficient document structure:** Place non-critical content at the end of the DOM, or load it asynchronously. The initial HTML payload should contain only the content necessary for the first paint. Below-the-fold content can be streamed in or lazy-loaded.

**Minimal complexity:** The number of DOM nodes in the document is a key performance metric. We monitor DOM size in performance budgets. A page with tens of thousands of nodes is unacceptable. Virtualization (windowing) is used for long lists to keep the DOM minimal, but the principle remains: don’t create DOM nodes that the user doesn’t need to see.

## HTML Security

The HTML we generate and serve is a security boundary. It must be robust against injection attacks and protect user data.

**Safe structure:** Never use `innerHTML` with unsanitized user input. Use a templating engine that auto-escapes output, or explicitly sanitize with a library like DOMPurify. Treat all user-generated content as malicious.

**Safe embedding:** If embedding third-party content, use `<iframe>` with the `sandbox` attribute. Restrict capabilities to the absolute minimum. The `<iframe>` should have a restrictive Content Security Policy.

**Safe forms:** Use CSRF tokens for all state-changing requests. Use HTTPS for all pages. The `action` and `method` attributes on forms ensure they work even without JavaScript, and browsers can provide basic security features like autocomplete.

**Safe user-generated content:** Rich text from users is rendered in a sandboxed environment. Markdown is converted to safe HTML. Any custom HTML in user content is heavily filtered. Never render raw HTML from users.

**Safe attributes:** Avoid inline event handlers (`onclick` in HTML). They are incompatible with Content Security Policy and separate behavior from structure. Use JavaScript event listeners added externally.

**Safe markup philosophy:** The HTML document is the outer shell of our application. It should be as simple and static as possible. All dynamic behavior is controlled by JavaScript and CSS, which are subject to their own security policies. The HTML itself should contain no secrets, no keys, and no logic—only structure and content.

## HTML Anti-Patterns

1. **Div soup.** Using `<div>` for everything: buttons, navigation, sections, headings. *Why:* It renders the page meaningless to assistive technology and makes the source unreadable. *Avoid:* Learn and use the full vocabulary of HTML5.

2. **Meaningless wrappers.** Adding extra `<div>` layers solely to apply CSS or JavaScript hooks. *Avoid:* Style the semantic element directly, or use a single, necessary wrapper.

3. **Deep nesting.** Nesting elements 10+ levels deep. *Why:* It’s a performance and readability nightmare. *Avoid:* Keep the DOM shallow. Break complex UI into smaller components, but each component’s root should be a single semantic element.

4. **Incorrect heading hierarchy.** Skipping from `<h1>` to `<h3>`, or having multiple `<h1>`s. *Why:* It destroys the document outline for assistive technology. *Avoid:* Plan the heading structure before writing HTML. Use a heading map tool.

5. **Broken landmarks.** A page with no `<main>`, or multiple `<main>`s, or navigation outside `<nav>`. *Why:* Screen reader users cannot efficiently navigate. *Avoid:* Include a single `<main>` and wrap all major navigation in `<nav>`.

6. **Buttons as divs.** `<div onclick="...">` instead of `<button>`. *Why:* It’s not keyboard-focusable by default, doesn’t respond to Enter/Space, and has no semantic role. *Avoid:* Use a `<button>`. If you absolutely cannot, add `role="button"`, `tabindex="0"`, and keyboard handlers—but you can use a `<button>`.

7. **Clickable spans.** `<span onclick="...">` used as a link or button. *Why:* Same problems as div buttons, plus inline elements for interactive controls are bad practice. *Avoid:* Use `<a>` or `<button>`.

8. **Missing labels on inputs.** An input field with no associated `<label>`, using only a `placeholder`. *Why:* Placeholder disappears on focus, and screen readers don’t treat placeholder as a label. *Avoid:* Always use `<label for="...">`.

9. **Duplicated IDs.** Two elements with the same `id` attribute. *Why:* It’s invalid HTML and breaks JavaScript and label associations. *Avoid:* Use a unique ID per page, or rely on classes and other selectors.

10. **Invalid nesting.** Putting a `<div>` inside a `<span>`, a `<button>` inside an `<a>`, or an interactive element inside an interactive element. *Why:* It violates the HTML spec and can cause unpredictable behavior. *Avoid:* Understand the content models of elements.

11. **Presentation-focused markup.** Using classes like `.text-red` or `.font-large` instead of `.error-message` or `.page-title`. *Why:* It ties styling to the HTML, making redesigns and theming hard. *Avoid:* Use semantic class names.

12. **Using `<br>` for spacing.** Multiple `<br>` tags to create vertical space between paragraphs or sections. *Why:* It mixes presentation with content and is not accessible. *Avoid:* Use CSS margins on semantic elements.

13. **Tables for layout.** Using `<table>` to create a multi-column page layout. *Why:* It’s a 1990s technique, terrible for accessibility, and makes responsive design impossible. *Avoid:* Use CSS Grid or Flexbox.

14. **Empty links.** `<a href="#">` without meaningful content. *Why:* It’s a dead end for keyboard and screen reader users. *Avoid:* Use a `<button>` if it triggers an action. If it must be a link, provide a real destination or a clear label.

15. **Images without `alt`.** *Why:* Screen readers cannot convey the image’s information. *Avoid:* Always include `alt`, empty string for decorative images.

16. **Uncaptioned media.** A video or audio player with no transcript or captions. *Why:* Deaf and hard-of-hearing users cannot access the content. *Avoid:* Provide a transcript and captions.

17. **Over-reliance on ARIA.** Using `role="button"` on a `<div>` instead of just using a `<button>`. *Why:* It’s more code for a worse result. *Avoid:* Use native elements first. ARIA only when necessary.

18. **Using `tabindex="0"` on non-interactive elements without good reason.** It forces a focusable element that does nothing interactive. *Why:* It confuses keyboard users. *Avoid:* Only interactive elements should be in the tab order.

19. **Non-descriptive link text.** “Read more,” “click here,” or URLs as link text. *Why:* Screen reader users get a list of meaningless links. *Avoid:* Write descriptive link text that indicates the destination or action.

20. **Missing `<title>` or a generic `<title>`.** Using “Untitled” or the same title for every page. *Why:* It’s a critical navigation failure. *Avoid:* Use a unique, descriptive title for every page.

21. **Over-using `<section>` without headings.** A `<section>` should have an accessible name, usually from a heading inside it or via `aria-label`. An unlabeled section is a semantic dead end. *Avoid:* Add a heading or use a `<div>` if it’s purely a styling wrapper.

22. **Using `role="presentation"` on a semantic element that should be conveyed.** This strips the element’s semantics, often incorrectly. *Avoid:* Understand the purpose of the role.

23. **Ignoring the document outline.** Throwing headings around without a logical plan. *Avoid:* Use a heading structure that reflects the content’s importance.

24. **Auto-playing media with sound.** It startles users and may violate accessibility guidelines. *Avoid:* Always start media muted and let the user unmute.

25. **CSS-only tooltips** that don’t appear on keyboard focus. *Avoid:* Use a proper tooltip component that works on hover and focus, or ensure the information is available elsewhere.

26. **Mega-menus without proper keyboard handling.** *Avoid:* Follow the WAI-ARIA menu pattern.

27. **Modal dialogs without focus trapping.** *Avoid:* Use the `<dialog>` element or a well-tested library.

28. **Forms that submit on Enter in a textarea unexpectedly.** *Avoid:* Use `<button type="submit">` correctly, and don’t capture Enter unless the form is meant to be submitted.

29. **Hiding content from all users with `aria-hidden="true"` when it’s supposed to be visible.** *Avoid:* Use `display: none` or `hidden` for hidden content; `aria-hidden` is for presentational removal for screen readers only.

30. **Inconsistent heading styles (e.g., a `<h2>` that’s bigger than the `<h1>`).** The visual style should match the semantic rank, or the semantics should match the visual hierarchy. *Avoid:* Size doesn’t determine the heading level; meaning does. But the visual hierarchy should roughly align.

31. **Using `<b>` and `<i>` for styling when `<strong>` and `<em>` are semantically correct.** *Avoid:* Use semantic tags; style with CSS if you just want bold/italic.

32. **Leaving out the `lang` attribute on the `<html>` element.** *Avoid:* Always include it. It’s a WCAG requirement.

33. **Not using `title` on `<iframe>`.** *Avoid:* Always provide a descriptive title.

34. **Using `maxlength` as the only validation.** It can be easily bypassed; server-side validation is also required. *Avoid:* Always validate server-side.

35. **Putting content in `data-*` attributes that should be visible.** Data attributes are for scripts, not for hiding data from the UI.

36. **Ignoring the `autocomplete` attribute on form fields.** It helps users fill forms quickly and is an accessibility feature. *Avoid:* Use appropriate `autocomplete` values (e.g., `off`, `on`, `name`, `email`).

37. **Using CSS to reorder content that should be in a different logical order.** *Avoid:* Fix the DOM order if the content needs to be read in a certain sequence.

38. **Empty `<div>` or `<span>` used as a visual spacer.** *Avoid:* Use CSS `margin` or `gap` on the parent.

39. **Excessive comments that describe obvious markup.** *Avoid:* Comment only why, not what. The semantic HTML should be self-explanatory.

40. **Hardcoding URLs without proper encoding.** Use `encodeURI` or similar for dynamic URLs.

41. **Allowing user input to set `id` or `class` names.** It can break JavaScript or inject unexpected styles.

42. **Using `!important` in inline styles, which is a CSS issue but often starts from bad HTML practices (no class to target).** *Avoid:* Use classes.

43. **Relying on `<meta>` keywords for SEO.** They are obsolete. *Avoid:* Focus on content and semantics.

44. **Putting block-level elements inside inline elements (e.g., `<span><div>...</div></span>`).** It’s invalid.

45. **Using `<iframe>` without a `sandbox` or with overly permissive sandbox attributes for user content.** *Avoid:* Limit to `allow-scripts` etc., only what’s needed.

46. **Creating a skip-to-content link that doesn’t work because the target isn’t focusable.** *Avoid:* The target must have `tabindex="-1"` and an `id`.

47. **Using the `style` attribute extensively.** It’s inline CSS, which is hard to override and breaks separation of concerns. *Avoid:* Use classes.

48. **Not closing void elements (like `<br>`) in a codebase, leading to inconsistency.** *Avoid:* Pick a style (XHTML or HTML5) and enforce with a linter.

49. **Using `<font>` or `<center>` or other deprecated elements.** *Avoid:* They are obsolete.

50. **Failing to provide a fallback for `<object>` or `<embed>`.** *Avoid:* Use nested content for fallback.

51. **Assuming all users have JavaScript enabled.** *Avoid:* The core content should be rendered server-side.

52. **Placing `<script>` tags that block rendering at the top of the page.** *Avoid:* Use `defer` or `async`, or place before closing `</body>`.

53. **Using a `<div>` to wrap every single line of text.** *Avoid:* Use `<p>` for paragraphs.

54. **Ignoring the `accesskey` attribute entirely, but also using it poorly.** *Avoid:* `accesskey` can conflict with browser/OS shortcuts; use with caution.

55. **Using `target="_blank"` without `rel="noopener noreferrer"` for security.**

56. **Not providing a clear error message for form validation, only changing the border color.** *Avoid:* Include text, associate with `aria-describedby`.

57. **Using `title` attribute for important information that should be visible.** *Why:* It’s not accessible to keyboard-only or touch users. *Avoid:* Only use `title` for supplemental information; critical content should be visible.

58. **Using the `<fieldset>` element without a `<legend>`.** It’s incomplete.

59. **Duplicating content for screen reader users (e.g., using `.sr-only` and `aria-label` together).** *Avoid:* One descriptive method is enough.

60. **Creating a document that is just a single `<div id="root">` with all content rendered by JavaScript.** *Why:* It’s meaningless without JS, not SEO-friendly, and slow to render. *Avoid:* Server-side render critical HTML. Progressive enhancement.

## HTML Review Framework

Every page, template, and component’s HTML must be reviewed against these questions.

1. **Does this document communicate meaning without CSS?** *Purpose:* Verify semantic integrity. *Reason:* The structure must stand alone. *Evaluation:* Disable CSS; read the raw page. *Acceptance:* It’s a coherent, structured document.

2. **Can a screen reader user understand the page’s structure and complete all tasks?** *Purpose:* Accessibility. *Evaluation:* Test with a screen reader. *Acceptance:* All tasks completable.

3. **Is the heading hierarchy logical and complete?** *Purpose:* Outline quality. *Evaluation:* Check heading levels. *Acceptance:* No skipped levels, single `<h1>`.

4. **Is the reading order logical?** *Purpose:* DOM order. *Evaluation:* Review source order vs. visual layout. *Acceptance:* Source order matches logical sequence.

5. **Are all interactive elements native (<button>, <a>) or have proper ARIA roles and keyboard handling?** *Purpose:* Interaction accessibility. *Acceptance:* Native elements used; custom widgets fully accessible.

6. **Do all form controls have associated labels?** *Purpose:* Usability. *Acceptance:* Every input has a visible and programmatic label.

7. **Are all images, videos, and icons correctly described or marked as decorative?** *Purpose:* Media accessibility. *Acceptance:* `alt` text present, complex images have text alternatives, decorative images have empty `alt`.

8. **Is the document free of invalid HTML?** *Purpose:* Correctness. *Evaluation:* Run a validator. *Acceptance:* Zero errors.

9. **Are landmark elements used appropriately?** *Purpose:* Navigation. *Acceptance:* `<header>`, `<main>`, `<footer>`, and `<nav>` present and correctly placed.

10. **Is the DOM shallow, with minimal unnecessary wrapping elements?** *Purpose:* Performance and readability. *Acceptance:* No more than 3-4 levels of nesting for content areas.

11. **Are class names semantic and consistent with the naming convention?** *Purpose:* Maintainability. *Acceptance:* Follow BEM or similar; no presentational class names.

12. **Is inline CSS and JavaScript avoided?** *Purpose:* Separation of concerns. *Acceptance:* Only in exceptional, justified cases.

13. **Does the page have a unique, descriptive `<title>` and meta description?** *Purpose:* SEO and orientation. *Acceptance:* Title is specific; description is compelling.

14. **Is content visible and accessible without JavaScript?** *Purpose:* Progressive enhancement. *Evaluation:* Disable JS. *Acceptance:* Core content and links/forms work.

15. **Are data attributes used exclusively for JavaScript, not for CSS?** *Purpose:* Clean separation. *Acceptance:* No CSS rules targeting `[data-*]` unless it’s a state selector.

16. **Is `lang` attribute set on `<html>` and correctly on multi-language blocks?** *Purpose:* Internationalization. *Acceptance:* Present and correct.

17. **Are `<iframe>` elements titled and sandboxed appropriately?** *Purpose:* Security. *Acceptance:* Title present; sandbox with minimal permissions.

18. **Is there a skip-to-content link?** *Purpose:* Keyboard accessibility. *Acceptance:* A “Skip to main content” link is the first focusable element.

19. **Are tables used only for data, with proper headers and captions?** *Purpose:* Semantic correctness. *Acceptance:* No layout tables; data tables have `<caption>` and `<th scope>`.

20. **Are there any empty elements that exist purely for styling?** *Purpose:* DOM bloat. *Acceptance:* None; if an empty wrapper is necessary, it has a comment explaining why.

21. **Does the HTML use `role` attributes correctly, without conflicting with native semantics?** *Purpose:* ARIA correctness. *Acceptance:* No `role="button"` on a `<button>`.

22. **Are links descriptive and avoid “click here” text?** *Purpose:* Link usability. *Acceptance:* Link text explains the target.

23. **Is the `<dialog>` element used for modals, with correct `showModal()`?** *Purpose:* Modal accessibility. *Acceptance:* Native dialog or a fully compliant custom dialog.

24. **Are forms submittable with Enter?** *Purpose:* Standard behavior. *Acceptance:* Pressing Enter in an input submits the form.

25. **Is the `autocomplete` attribute set appropriately on inputs?** *Purpose:* Usability. *Acceptance:* Autofill works correctly.

26. **Is the page free of positive `tabindex` values?** *Purpose:* Natural tab order. *Acceptance:* No `tabindex` greater than 0.

27. **Is the `hidden` attribute or `display: none` used correctly for truly hidden content?** *Purpose:* Hide from all. *Acceptance:* Content not in the current view is hidden from all modalities.

28. **Are error messages for form fields programmatically associated with the fields?** *Purpose:* Error accessibility. *Acceptance:* `aria-describedby` links error to input.

29. **Is the document’s character encoding declared and correct?** *Purpose:* Text rendering. *Acceptance:* `<meta charset="utf-8">` is the first element in `<head>`.

30. **Are there any `<meta>` tags that are unnecessary or outdated?** *Purpose:* Clean head. *Acceptance:* Only essential meta tags.

31. **Does the page work when resized to 320px width?** *Purpose:* Responsive layout. *Acceptance:* No horizontal scroll, content readable. Though HTML structure is not solely responsible, proper use of viewport meta and semantic structure aids.

32. **Are long code blocks and preformatted text correctly wrapped in `<pre>` and `<code>`?** *Purpose:* Semantic display. *Acceptance:* Yes.

33. **Is the `title` attribute used only for supplemental information and not for critical content?** *Purpose:* Accessibility. *Acceptance:* No critical information only in a `title`.

34. **Are there any missing required attributes on elements (e.g., `alt` on `<img>`, `type` on `<button>`)?** *Purpose:* Valid markup. *Acceptance:* All attributes present.

35. **Are IDs unique within the document?** *Purpose:* Uniqueness. *Acceptance:* No duplicate `id`s.

36. **Does the component’s root element communicate its purpose?** *Purpose:* Component semantics. *Acceptance:* A card component’s root is `<article>` or `<section>` with a heading.

37. **Are list elements used for groups of similar items?** *Purpose:* List semantics. *Acceptance:* Navigation, feature lists, etc. use `<ul>`/`<ol>`.

38. **Is the `<output>` element used where appropriate?** *Purpose:* Semantic feedback. *Acceptance:* Live regions for dynamic output are `<output>`.

39. **Does the HTML avoid using deprecated elements like `<center>`, `<font>`, `<marquee>`?** *Purpose:* Modern standards. *Acceptance:* No deprecated elements.

40. **Are `aria-live` regions used for dynamically updating content, and are they polite?** *Purpose:* Real-time updates. *Acceptance:* Toasts and status messages are in `aria-live="polite"` regions.

41. **Is the `<main>` element a direct child of `<body>` and not nested inside another landmark?** *Purpose:* Proper landmark structure. *Acceptance:* Yes.

42. **Is there only one `<main>` per page?** *Purpose:* Standard. *Acceptance:* Single main.

43. **Are `<article>` and `<section>` used correctly, not as generic wrappers?** *Purpose:* Semantic precision. *Acceptance:* Each has a thematic purpose and usually a heading.

44. **Do all interactive elements have a visible focus style?** *Purpose:* Keyboard usability. *Acceptance:* Focus outline is present and custom.

45. **Are there any `<div>` or `<span>` elements that could be replaced by a more semantic element?** *Purpose:* Semantic upgrade. *Evaluation:* Search for `<div>`; can it be `<section>`, `<p>`, `<blockquote>`? *Acceptance:* Minimal divs.

46. **Is the HTML formatted consistently (indentation, line breaks)?** *Purpose:* Readability. *Acceptance:* Enforced by Prettier.

47. **Are comments used sparingly and explain “why,” not “what”?** *Purpose:* Documentation. *Acceptance:* Comments are meaningful.

48. **Does the page avoid using `document.write()` or other DOM injection methods that break the parser?** *Purpose:* Performance. *Acceptance:* Not present.

49. **Are there any `<script>` tags without `defer` or `async` that block rendering?** *Purpose:* Load performance. *Acceptance:* Scripts are deferred.

50. **Is the page free of XSS vulnerabilities through proper escaping?** *Purpose:* Security. *Acceptance:* User content is escaped/sanitized.

51. **Is the HTML generated by the AI or template engine also adhering to these standards?** *Purpose:* Consistency. *Acceptance:* Same quality.

52. **Could a new developer understand the page structure in under 5 minutes?** *Purpose:* Onboarding. *Acceptance:* Yes.

53. **Are the Open Graph and Twitter Card meta tags present for shareable pages?** *Purpose:* Social sharing. *Acceptance:* Present on public pages.

54. **Is the `<noscript>` tag present and useful?** *Purpose:* Fallback. *Acceptance:* Content explains the need for JavaScript.

55. **Does the HTML structure avoid “flickering” due to late-loading styles?** *Purpose:* Stability. *Acceptance:* Inline critical CSS or use proper loading techniques, but the HTML itself should not cause FOUC.

56. **Is the ARIA state (`aria-expanded`, `aria-selected`, etc.) kept in sync with visual state?** *Purpose:* Accessibility. *Acceptance:* Dynamic states are updated.

57. **Is the `role` attribute used to clarify a component’s purpose only when the native element is ambiguous?** *Purpose:* ARIA minimalism. *Acceptance:* No redundant roles.

58. **Are `tabindex="-1"` and focus management correctly used for modal and navigation flows?** *Purpose:* Focus control. *Acceptance:* Modal traps focus; closing returns focus.

59. **Does the page load and render progressively, with important content first?** *Purpose:* Perceived performance. *Acceptance:* Content appears in logical order.

60. **Is the HTML a source of pride for the engineering team?** *Purpose:* Quality. *Acceptance:* The team believes it represents the best possible semantic foundation.

## HTML Quality Levels

**Poor HTML:** A tag soup of `<div>`s, missing semantics, broken accessibility, and invalid markup. It is a liability that causes constant bugs and user frustration. It is never acceptable.

**Average HTML:** Functional but generic. It uses some semantic elements but relies heavily on `<div>` and `<span>`. Accessibility is partially addressed but with gaps. It works, but doesn’t communicate intent clearly.

**Good HTML:** Clean, semantic, accessible, and valid. It uses the correct elements for their purpose, has a logical heading structure, and is well-formatted. It passes automated accessibility checks. This is the minimum bar for any production page in Zenuxs.

**Excellent HTML:** Perfectly semantic, beautifully structured, and a model of progressive enhancement. It is so clear that a developer can read the raw HTML and immediately grasp the page’s architecture. It sets the standard for the team.

**World-Class HTML:** The kind of HTML that is used as an example in industry talks and documentation. It is faultless in its semantics, performance, and accessibility. It elegantly solves complex document structures with minimal markup.

**Zenuxs HTML:** The unique blend of extreme semantic precision, developer-centric readability, and a deep commitment to the web platform. It is the HTML equivalent of a precision-engineered instrument. Zenuxs will always ship Good or better, and will never knowingly ship Average or Poor.

## Future-Proof HTML

HTML is the most stable part of the web stack. By investing deeply in clean, semantic HTML, we protect the platform’s long-term viability.

**How HTML survives framework changes:** When the React-to-Svelte migration happens, the HTML templates are the easiest to port because they are just standard markup. A `<button>` remains a `<button>`. Components that wrap native elements with minimal custom logic can be re-implemented in any framework. The HTML stays the same; only the binding layer changes.

**How HTML survives redesigns:** A redesign changes the CSS, not the meaning of the content. If the HTML describes the content’s role (a heading, a list, a form), a designer can restyle it completely without touching the HTML. The separation of concerns ensures that visual trends can evolve while the structural integrity remains.

**How HTML remains maintainable:** The semantic structure is self-documenting. New team members, even those unfamiliar with the current framework, can understand the page by reading the HTML. This reduces the bus factor and makes long-term maintenance possible.

**How HTML remains understandable for future AI agents:** As AI models become more capable of parsing and contributing to codebases, clean, standard HTML allows them to understand the interface’s structure immediately. An AI trained on web standards can navigate a well-crafted Zenuxs document far more effectively than a spaghetti of custom attributes and `<div>`s.

**How HTML protects long-term product quality:** The web platform is committed to backward compatibility. HTML written today will render in browsers twenty years from now. By sticking to standards and avoiding proprietary or framework-specific patterns, we ensure that Zenuxs’s interfaces are durable. This is an act of professional stewardship.

## HTML Benchmark Framework

We draw inspiration from organizations renowned for their front-end engineering quality, adapting principles, not copying implementations.

**Google:** Their emphasis on performance and the Core Web Vitals has shaped how they write HTML: critical CSS inlined, scripts deferred, minimal render-blocking resources. *Principle:* Performance is a feature of the HTML structure. *What not to copy:* The often machine-generated, unreadable HTML of their complex apps. *How Zenuxs adapts:* We prioritize performance but keep the source human-readable.

**Mozilla (MDN):** They are the guardians of web standards and semantic HTML. Their documentation exemplifies perfect semantic structure. *Principle:* Use every HTML element correctly and to its fullest potential. *What not to copy:* The sometimes overly academic approach that might prioritize semantics over pragmatic component design. *How Zenuxs adapts:* We blend semantics with the practical needs of a component-based application, finding the right semantic element even within a component tree.

**W3C:** The source of the specifications. *Principle:* Conformance to standards is the baseline. *What not to copy:* The spec’s abstractness; we need concrete patterns. *How Zenuxs adapts:* We implement the specs fully, building our patterns on the solid ground of standards.

**Apple:** Their HTML, especially on their product pages, is a masterclass in accessibility and performance. They use semantic HTML, landmarks, and text alternatives beautifully. *Principle:* Accessibility is a design value, not a bolt-on. *What not to copy:* Their specific, often marketing-focused page structures. *How Zenuxs adapts:* We apply the same rigor to our interfaces, ensuring every developer interaction is accessible.

**GitHub:** Their front-end is a complex application built with Web Components and a robust semantic core. They maintain keyboard accessibility and use landmarks effectively. *Principle:* A complex, interactive application can still be highly semantic. *What not to copy:* The deep, framework-specific attribute soup (though they manage it well). *How Zenuxs adapts:* We aim to keep our rendered HTML as clean as possible, even with a complex framework, by carefully designing our components.

**Vercel:** Their marketing and documentation pages are beautifully crafted with semantic HTML and stunning performance. *Principle:* Design and performance can coexist in perfect HTML. *What not to copy:* The highly Next.js-specific data attributes for preloading, etc., which are implementation details. *How Zenuxs adapts:* We use similar techniques where appropriate, but abstract them behind our component model.

**MDN Web Docs:** (Repeating the point) Their own site is the living example of what semantic HTML should be. It’s the gold standard for a content-heavy, accessible, well-structured site.

## The Zenuxs HTML Standard

HTML excellence at Zenuxs is the practice of writing markup that is so clear, so semantic, and so robust that it becomes the definitive source of truth for the entire interface, outlasting any framework or design trend. It is the craft of building for the web platform itself.

Contributors write HTML as if they were authoring a public specification. They choose elements for their meaning, structure documents with intention, and treat every attribute as a commitment. They understand that their work is the interface not just for visual users, but for screen readers, search engines, and AI agents.

AI agents generate HTML that adheres to these same standards. They do not output div soup. When prompted to create a component, they output the most semantic, accessible, and minimal HTML structure possible, because they have been trained on this constitution. The AI is a junior developer who follows the Zenuxs HTML standard to the letter.

Reviewers evaluate HTML with a critical eye. They check the document outline, validate the markup, test with a screen reader, and challenge every unnecessary element. They are the guardians of the semantic web. A code review for HTML is as rigorous as a code review for security or performance.

Future engineers maintain HTML by respecting the existing structure. When adding a feature, they fit it into the semantic hierarchy, not append it as a new `<div>`. They refactor HTML with the same care they would a database schema, ensuring backward compatibility and maintaining the integrity of the document.

The Zenuxs HTML Standard is the foundation of our engineering culture. It is a statement that we care about the medium as much as the message, that we respect every user, and that we build for the long term. Every Zenuxs HTML document is a testament to that belief. It is not just code; it is a permanent, universal, and accessible work of engineering.

# CSS Standards

## CSS Philosophy

CSS is not a paintbrush; it is the architectural specification of the visual language. Every rule, selector, and value in a Zenuxs stylesheet is a deliberate design decision encoded for the browser. The quality of CSS directly determines the perceived quality of the product—whether an interface feels solid and trustworthy or fragile and inconsistent. At Zenuxs, we treat CSS with the same engineering rigor as our backend services. It is architecture, not decoration.

CSS communicates engineering quality. A stylesheet riddled with magic numbers, deeply nested selectors, and `!important` overrides broadcasts chaos. A stylesheet built on a system of design tokens, with a flat specificity graph and clear naming conventions, broadcasts precision. When a developer inspects an element and sees a logical, well-organized cascade, they trust the platform. When they see a tangled mess, they instinctively doubt the underlying codebase.

CSS must remain predictable. A change to a component’s styles must not cascade into unexpected breakage in unrelated parts of the interface. Predictability is achieved through low specificity, scoped styles, and a strict naming methodology. A developer modifying a `.card` class should never accidentally restyle a `.card` inside a modal. The scope of every rule must be transparent and intentional.

CSS must remain maintainable. Stylesheets that are maintained over years, by many hands, tend toward entropy unless disciplined. Maintainable CSS is well-organized, well-commented, and built on shared abstractions (tokens, utilities, mixins). It is easy to delete dead code because styles are co-located with components. It is easy to onboard new contributors because the naming conventions are self-explanatory.

CSS should express systems, not isolated rules. A single design decision—like the primary spacing unit—should propagate across the entire platform through a variable. A change to the brand color should be a one-line edit, not a thousand find-and-replace operations. The stylesheet is the executable form of the design system. It must faithfully reflect the system’s abstractions.

CSS should support designers and engineers equally. For designers, CSS must be a faithful translation of their visual intent, pixel-perfect and alive with motion. For engineers, CSS must be a logical, debuggable, and performant layer. A CSS architecture that frustrates either party is a failure. The styling system is the bridge between design and engineering, and it must be strong.

## CSS Engineering Principles

1. **Describe systems, not pages.** CSS should be organized around reusable patterns (components, layouts, utilities) rather than single pages. A rule that only works on the “About Us” page is a liability. *Reason:* Reusability reduces duplication and ensures visual consistency. *Guidance:* If a style is used only once, question whether it should be a utility or simply a component variant.

2. **CSS must remain scalable.** The styling system must function just as well with 20 components as with 2,000. It must avoid specificity creep, naming collisions, and file-size bloat. *Reason:* The platform will grow; the CSS must accommodate that growth without requiring a rewrite. *Guidance:* Use BEM or similar scoped naming, avoid global resets that are too broad, and leverage tooling for dead code elimination.

3. **Predictability over brevity.** A clear, slightly verbose class name is better than a cryptic abbreviation. A predictable cascade is better than a clever, fragile override. *Reason:* CSS is read far more than it is written. *Guidance:* Use full words in class names (`.button--primary`, not `.btn-p`).

4. **Variables over magic values.** Any value that is reused—colors, spacing, radii, shadows—must be defined as a CSS custom property or preprocessor variable. *Reason:* A single source of truth makes changes safe and fast. *Guidance:* Never hardcode a hex code or pixel value more than once in the codebase; abstract it into a design token.

5. **Layout before decoration.** The structural skeleton (grid, flexbox, spacing) must be solid before any visual flourishes (gradients, shadows, animations) are added. *Reason:* Decoration without a sound layout collapses under different content or viewport sizes. *Guidance:* Build a layout that works in plain black and white, then apply visual style.

6. **Spacing before styling.** Consistent spacing is the most powerful tool for visual hierarchy. Spacing values (margin, padding, gap) must come from a defined scale. *Reason:* Random spacing creates visual clutter. *Guidance:* Use a limited set of spacing tokens (e.g., 4, 8, 12, 16, 24, 32, 48, 64px) and apply them universally.

7. **Typography before effects.** A well-set type system is the foundation of readable, professional UI. Define font families, sizes, weights, and line heights as tokens before any other visual styling. *Reason:* Typography carries the majority of information. *Guidance:* Apply a consistent typographic scale and use semantic font tokens.

8. **Never fight the browser.** Leverage native browser capabilities like scrolling, focus management, and form controls. Overriding them with JavaScript-driven custom implementations must be a last resort. *Reason:* The browser is a highly optimized, accessible rendering engine. Fighting it introduces bugs and performance problems. *Guidance:* Before building a custom scrollbar, try styling the native one. Before building a custom select, try enhancing the native one.

9. **CSS should age gracefully.** Write CSS that will still make sense five years from now. Avoid trendy hacks that rely on obscure browser quirks. *Reason:* The codebase is a long-term asset. *Guidance:* Favor standard, widely-supported properties. Use PostCSS for future syntax today, but avoid proprietary features.

10. **Low specificity is a virtue.** Keep selectors as flat as possible, ideally a single class. Avoid chaining multiple classes or elements unless necessary to overcome a framework limitation. *Reason:* High specificity leads to specificity wars, where the only solution is more specificity or `!important`. *Guidance:* Maximum specificity for a component’s root is 0,1,0 (one class). Use BEM for sub-elements and modifiers.

11. **Scope styles to components.** A component’s CSS must not leak out and style sibling components, nor should it be vulnerable to external styles leaking in (except for intentional theming). *Reason:* Encapsulation enables fearless refactoring. *Guidance:* Use CSS Modules, scoped styles in frameworks, or strict BEM naming to enforce isolation.

12. **Use consistent selector conventions.** The entire codebase uses the same methodology (e.g., BEM, or a utility-first approach) without mixing styles. *Reason:* A consistent mental model reduces cognitive load. *Guidance:* Document the chosen convention, and enforce it with a linter.

13. **Separate structure from skin.** Properties like color, background, and border (skin) should be separated from layout properties like width, margin, and padding (structure) where possible. *Reason:* A component’s layout might be reused with a different visual theme. *Guidance:* Use semantic background/color tokens; keep layout classes separate from color theming.

14. **Composition over inheritance.** Favor composing multiple small, single-purpose classes or components rather than building deep, inherited style hierarchies. *Reason:* Inheritance is fragile and hard to debug. Composition is explicit. *Guidance:* Use utility classes for small visual adjustments, but avoid inheritance of complex styles.

15. **Every pixel has a source of truth.** A value’s origin—a design token, a component variable, an inline override—must be traceable. *Reason:* Debugging “why is this 14px?” should not require guesswork. *Guidance:* Use CSS custom properties to surface variable origins in DevTools.

16. **Modifiers over context-dependent styles.** A component’s visual variation (e.g., a primary button, a large card) is expressed through a modifier class (`.button--primary`), not through a parent class (`.header .button`). *Reason:* Context-dependent styles create hidden coupling and make components non-reusable. *Guidance:* A component should look the same regardless of where it is placed, unless a modifier is explicitly applied.

17. **Responsive design is a system, not a set of breakpoints.** Use fluid techniques (`clamp()`, `minmax()`, percentages) to avoid reliance on hard-coded breakpoints. Breakpoints are for layout re-structuring, not for every minor adjustment. *Reason:* A fluid layout adapts gracefully to any viewport, including ones that don’t exist yet. *Guidance:* Think in terms of “containers” that reflow, not “pages” that break.

18. **Avoid magic numbers.** A value like `margin-top: 23px` is a magic number. It has no relationship to the spacing scale and no clear intent. *Reason:* Magic numbers erode the system’s integrity. *Guidance:* All values must come from a defined token or be a calculated value (e.g., `calc(var(--space-md) / 2)`).

19. **Z-index is managed centrally.** Never scatter random `z-index` values. A central `z-index` scale (e.g., 0, 100, 200, 1000) defines the layering model. *Reason:* Random `z-index` values lead to stacking wars that are impossible to win. *Guidance:* Document the scale and enforce it via a linter.

20. **Motion is expressed in tokens.** Animation durations, easing curves, and delays are defined as CSS custom properties. No hardcoded `0.3s ease-out` in component files. *Reason:* Consistent motion is a brand signature. *Guidance:* Use `var(--motion-duration-normal)` etc.

21. **Colors are semantic tokens.** Never use raw hex values in component styles. Use `var(--color-surface-primary)`, `var(--color-text-error)`. *Reason:* Enables dark mode, high-contrast mode, and theming. *Guidance:* Abstract all colors into a token layer that maps to themes.

22. **Typography is a tokenized scale.** Font sizes, weights, line heights, and font families are all variables. *Reason:* Maintains consistent type rhythm and enables global adjustments. *Guidance:* Use a scale like `--font-size-body`, `--font-size-heading-lg`.

23. **Shadows and elevation are tokens.** Use a small set of shadow tokens (`--shadow-sm`, `--shadow-md`, `--shadow-lg`) to create a consistent sense of depth. *Reason:* Inconsistent shadows look sloppy. *Guidance:* Define each token with multiple layered box-shadows for realism.

24. **Border radius is a limited set of tokens.** No component decides its own border radius; it picks from a scale (`--radius-sm`, `--radius-md`, `--radius-full`). *Reason:* Cohesive visual language. *Guidance:* Limit to 3-4 radius values.

25. **Use CSS Grid and Flexbox for layout.** Float-based layouts are forbidden. Grid handles two-dimensional layouts; Flexbox handles one-dimensional alignment. *Reason:* They are the modern, powerful, and correct tools for layout. *Guidance:* Master both; use the right tool for the job.

26. **Use logical properties for internationalization.** Use `margin-inline-start`, `padding-block`, `border-inline-end`, etc., instead of `left`/`right`/`top`/`bottom`. *Reason:* Automatically adapts to RTL languages. *Guidance:* Enable logical properties in your CSS reset and linter.

27. **Minimize repaints and reflows.** Understand which properties trigger layout, paint, and composite. Prefer `transform` and `opacity` for animations. *Reason:* Jank-free performance. *Guidance:* Profile animations. Use `will-change` sparingly and only when needed.

28. **Use `rem` and `em` for scalable sizing.** Font-relative units respect user preferences. *Reason:* Accessibility. *Guidance:* Use `rem` for font sizes and spacing; `em` for component-relative sizing.

29. **Never use `!important` as a crutch.** If a style is being overridden, find the root specificity conflict and resolve it, rather than escalating with `!important`. *Reason:* `!important` breaks the cascade and makes further overrides impossible. *Guidance:* The only acceptable use is for user-agent style overrides in a reset, or extremely rare utility classes like `.hidden`.

30. **Keep selectors simple.** The browser evaluates selectors from right to left; complex selectors are slower and harder to maintain. *Reason:* Performance and maintainability. *Guidance:* A class selector is optimal. Avoid deeply nested or universal selectors.

31. **Use a consistent CSS reset.** A single, well-maintained reset (e.g., a custom one based on modern best practices) normalizes browser defaults. No ad-hoc resets scattered across files. *Reason:* Consistent baseline. *Guidance:* Document the reset and don’t add to it without review.

32. **Document complex or non-obvious rules.** A comment explaining why a certain z-index is needed or why a specific `overflow` is hidden prevents future developers from “fixing” a deliberate workaround. *Reason:* Intent preservation. *Guidance:* Comment the “why,” not the “what.”

33. **Avoid styling IDs.** IDs have extremely high specificity and should be reserved for JavaScript hooks or anchor targets. *Reason:* Prevents specificity inflation. *Guidance:* Use classes for all styling.

34. **Isolate third-party component styles.** When a library’s styles must be overridden, do so in a dedicated file with a clear comment, and use a wrapper class to scope the overrides. *Reason:* Prevents leakage. *Guidance:* Never edit library CSS files directly.

35. **Favor CSS custom properties over preprocessor variables for dynamic theming.** Preprocessor variables are static after compile; custom properties can respond to runtime conditions (like theme). *Reason:* True dynamic styling. *Guidance:* Use preprocessor variables for build-time constants, custom properties for runtime variables.

36. **Use feature queries (`@supports`) for progressive enhancement.** Use new CSS features with a fallback when support is lacking. *Reason:* The platform runs on various browsers; we must not break the experience for users on older versions. *Guidance:* Write the fallback first, then enhance with `@supports`.

37. **Avoid over-using `position: absolute`.** Absolute positioning removes elements from the flow and often leads to brittle, overlapping layouts. *Reason:* Flow layout is more robust and accessible. *Guidance:* Use absolute positioning only for intentional overlays (tooltips, modals).

38. **Never use `*` universal selector for heavy resets.** It can cause performance issues on large DOM trees. *Reason:* It touches every single element. *Guidance:* Use a targeted reset or rely on inherited properties.

39. **Responsive images use `max-width: 100%` and `height: auto`.** This is a basic, non-negotiable rule for all `<img>` elements. *Reason:* Prevents overflow. *Guidance:* Include in the global reset.

40. **Hide content accessibly.** Use a `.sr-only` utility class for content that should be visible to screen readers but hidden visually. Do not use `display: none` for this. *Reason:* `display: none` removes it from the accessibility tree. *Guidance:* Use the standard clip-pattern for screen-reader-only text.

41. **Focus styles are mandatory and beautiful.** Never use `:focus { outline: none; }` without providing a superior custom focus indicator. *Reason:* Keyboard accessibility. *Guidance:* Design a consistent, high-contrast focus ring as a global token.

42. **Print styles are considered.** For documentation and marketing pages, a print stylesheet hides navigation and adjusts typography for paper. *Reason:* Users print documentation. *Guidance:* Include `@media print` in the main stylesheet for relevant pages.

43. **Reduce motion is respected globally.** All animations and transitions are wrapped in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)` or use a global custom property that toggles based on the media query. *Reason:* Motion sensitivity. *Guidance:* Use a `--motion-duration` variable that is set to `0s` when reduced motion is preferred.

44. **Dark mode is a first-class citizen.** Every component is designed for and tested in both light and dark themes. The color token system must map seamlessly to both. *Reason:* Developer preference. *Guidance:* Use `prefers-color-scheme` media query or a theme toggle; all colors are tokens.

45. **Component variants are implemented with modifier classes or component props, not by overriding base styles with high specificity.** A primary button is a variant of a button; it adds styles, not fights them. *Reason:* Clear, additive styling. *Guidance:* `.button` has base styles; `.button--primary` adds or overrides using a single class.

46. **Avoid inline styles in JavaScript.** They are hard to override and break separation of concerns. *Reason:* Inline styles have maximum specificity and cannot be managed via CSS. *Guidance:* Use classes or CSS custom properties to change styles dynamically; keep the styling logic in CSS.

47. **Performance budgets include CSS metrics.** Total CSS size, number of unused rules, and selector complexity are tracked in CI. *Reason:* Bloated CSS degrades load time and rendering. *Guidance:* Use tools like PurgeCSS, and monitor stylesheet sizes.

48. **Never use `@import` in production CSS.** It creates blocking, sequential resource loads. *Reason:* Performance. *Guidance:* Use a bundler to combine stylesheets, or `<link>` tags.

49. **Grid and Flexbox gap is preferred over margin for spacing between items.** `gap` is clean, consistent, and works with the flow. *Reason:* It avoids margin collapse issues and provides uniform spacing. *Guidance:* Use `gap` in flex and grid containers; avoid old margin hacks.

50. **Use CSS `clamp()` for fluid typography and spacing.** It allows responsive scaling without media queries. *Reason:* Simpler, more maintainable fluid design. *Guidance:* `font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem)`.

51. **Avoid deeply nested Sass/SCSS unless it mirrors the DOM.** Nesting more than 3 levels deep results in overly specific, hard-to-override selectors. *Reason:* Specificity creep. *Guidance:* Use nesting for pseudo-classes and pseudo-elements, not for deeply nested DOM structures.

52. **Use `:where()` for low-specificity defaults.** It allows setting styles that are easily overridden by component styles without increasing specificity. *Reason:* Ideal for resets or base styles. *Guidance:* `:where(button) { ... }` for a base button style.

53. **Leverage CSS custom properties for component APIs.** A component can expose CSS variables (`--button-bg`) that parents can set to customize. *Reason:* Clean, encapsulated styling API. *Guidance:* Document the exposed variables.

54. **Avoid long selector chains.** `.header .nav .nav__list .nav__item .nav__link` is a maintenance nightmare. *Reason:* Fragile and slow. *Guidance:* Use a single, descriptive class: `.nav-link`.

55. **CSS is versioned alongside components.** Changes to a component’s visual style are part of the component’s release cycle and are reviewed as such. *Reason:* Visual changes can break layout and must be tested.

56. **Every new class must have a defined purpose and be documented in the component’s story.** No orphaned classes. *Reason:* Prevents dead CSS.

57. **Use `aspect-ratio` property for consistent media containers.** Replaces the old padding-top hack. *Reason:* Native browser support, simpler code.

58. **Handle text overflow gracefully.** Use `text-overflow: ellipsis` or `line-clamp` for truncating text, but always provide a way to view the full text (e.g., a tooltip). *Reason:* Readability and layout stability.

59. **CSS comments are used to demarcate major sections and explain non-obvious rules.** A well-commented stylesheet is a sign of a disciplined team. *Reason:* Knowledge transfer.

60. **Every pull request that changes CSS is reviewed by a front-end engineer with a focus on cascade, specificity, and performance.** Visual review is not enough. *Reason:* CSS is logic.

## Design Token Philosophy

Design tokens are the atomic visual values—colors, spacing, typography, shadows, radii—that constitute the Zenuxs visual language. They are the single source of truth for all visual attributes. Tokens are stored in a platform-agnostic format (e.g., JSON) and transformed into CSS custom properties, JavaScript constants, and design tool variables. This ensures that a change to a brand color is reflected consistently across code, design files, and documentation.
