Is the interface ready for the next interaction?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Mobile Navigation
Purpose

Provide accessible, hierarchical navigation optimized for mobile interaction. Mobile navigation organizes content for constrained viewports and touch interaction.

Trigger

Mobile viewport detected. Hamburger or navigation trigger activated.

Beginning

Navigation is collapsed. Only the trigger or minimal navigation is visible.

Transition

Navigation expands to fill the viewport or appropriate space. The expansion may be from the edge, from the top, or overlay content. Items are optimized for touch—larger touch targets, clear spacing, simple hierarchy.

Peak

Navigation is fully expanded. All items are visible. The user can navigate effectively.

Resolution

The navigation holds its expanded state until the user dismisses or selects. Interaction is smooth and responsive.

End State

Mobile navigation expanded. Ready for touch interaction.

Accessibility

Touch targets must be at least 44px. Keyboard navigation must be complete. Screen readers must announce the navigation state.

Performance

Smooth scrolling within navigation if needed. Avoid reflows.

Failure State

Navigation appears expanded instantly. Items interactive.

Review Questions

Is the navigation optimized for touch?

Are touch targets appropriately sized?

Is the hierarchy clear on mobile?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Search Expansion
Purpose

Reveal search functionality with emphasis and clarity. Search expansion signals that the system is ready to accept query input.

Trigger

Search trigger interaction. Keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+K or similar). Click.

Beginning

Search is collapsed—an icon or minimal input field is visible.

Transition

The search field expands to its full size. Focus is applied. Placeholder text or hints appear. The expansion communicates that search is now the primary interaction.

Peak

Search is fully expanded. Focused. Ready for input.

Resolution

The search stays expanded and focused until the user submits a query, clears the field, or dismisses search.

End State

Search expanded. Input focused. Ready for typing.

Accessibility

Focus must move to the search input. Placeholder text must be accessible. Screen readers must announce search mode.

Performance

Simple transforms. Focus management.

Failure State

Search appears expanded instantly. Input focused.

Review Questions

Does the expansion clearly communicate search readiness?

Is focus managed appropriately?

Is the interaction smooth and responsive?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Command Palette
Purpose

Provide comprehensive command access through a keyboard-driven interface. The command palette is the control center for the Zenuxs power user experience.

Trigger

Keyboard shortcut (Cmd+K or Ctrl+K). Click. Gesture.

Beginning

Command palette is hidden. The interface is in standard state.

Transition

The palette slides, fades, or scales into position. A backdrop may appear. Input is automatically focused. The transition communicates: "You are now in command mode."

Peak

Palette is fully visible. Input is focused. The user can type any command or search any function.

Resolution

The palette remains visible and active until the user selects a command, presses Escape, or clicks outside. Results update dynamically as the user types.

End State

Palette visible. Results displayed. Ready for selection.

Accessibility

Full keyboard navigation required. Focus management must be complete. Screen readers must announce palette mode and results. Escape key must close.

Performance

Efficient. Search results should update quickly. Use debouncing for input handling.

Failure State

Palette appears instantly. Input focused. Results may be delayed but still functional.

Review Questions

Is the palette accessible via keyboard?

Is focus managed correctly?

Are results displayed clearly and quickly?

Is the palette dismissible through expected interactions?

RECIPE: Sidebar Collapse
Purpose

Reduce sidebar width to maximize content area while preserving access to primary navigation. Sidebar collapse provides content focus without losing orientation.

Trigger

Collapse trigger interaction. Viewport size change. User preference.

Beginning

Sidebar is fully expanded. Icons and labels visible.

Transition

Sidebar width reduces. Icons may remain or shift. Labels fade or slide out of visibility. The transition communicates: "I'm condensing to give your content more space."

Peak

Sidebar reaches its collapsed width. Icons are visible. Labels are hidden (or available on hover).

Resolution

The sidebar remains collapsed until the user expands it or triggers hover expansion. Content area is larger.

End State

Sidebar collapsed. Icons visible. Content area expanded.

Accessibility

Icon-only navigation must include accessible labels. Hover expansion must be keyboard accessible. Screen readers must announce the state change.

Performance

Simple transforms. Avoid reflows.

Failure State

Sidebar appears collapsed instantly.

Review Questions

Does the collapse provide more content space?

Are icons sufficient for navigation without labels?

Is the collapse accessible via keyboard?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Sidebar Expand
Purpose

Restore sidebar to full width for comprehensive navigation access. Sidebar expansion provides full context and complete navigation labels.

Trigger

Expand trigger interaction. Hover on collapsed sidebar. User preference.

Beginning

Sidebar is collapsed. Icons visible, labels hidden.

Transition

Sidebar width increases. Labels fade or slide into visibility. The transition communicates: "Your full navigation is returning."

Peak

Sidebar reaches its expanded width. Icons and labels visible.

Resolution

Sidebar remains expanded until the user collapses it or triggers collapse.

End State

Sidebar expanded. Icons and labels visible. Full navigation accessible.

Accessibility

Focus management if expanding changes navigation. Screen readers must announce the state change.

Performance

Simple transforms. Avoid reflows.

Failure State

Sidebar appears expanded instantly.

Review Questions

Does the expansion provide full navigation context?

Is the transition smooth and graceful?

Is the expansion accessible via keyboard?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Dock Activation
Purpose

Reveal the application dock or taskbar with clarity and hierarchy. Dock activation provides access to key applications and system functions.

Trigger

System interaction. Keyboard shortcut. Gesture. Hover.

Beginning

Dock is hidden or minimized. Applications are not visible.

Transition

Dock slides, scales, or fades into position. Applications appear with staggered timing. The transition communicates: "Your applications are ready."

Peak

Dock is fully visible. Applications arranged. Indicators active.

Resolution

Dock persists until hidden. Applications are interactive.

End State

Dock visible. Applications accessible. Ready for interaction.

Accessibility

Touch targets must be appropriately sized. Keyboard navigation required. Screen readers must announce dock state.

Performance

Smooth animations. GPU acceleration.

Failure State

Dock appears instantly. Applications accessible.

Review Questions

Does the dock activation provide efficient application access?

Is the layout clear and organized?

Is the transition smooth and responsive?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Breadcrumb Transition
Purpose

Communicate navigational hierarchy and location through breadcrumb updates. Breadcrumb transitions provide orientation and context during navigation.

Trigger

Navigation to new location. Page change. Section entry.

Beginning

Breadcrumbs show previous location. Current location not yet indicated.

Transition

Breadcrumbs update to show new hierarchy. Items may shift, fade, or slide. The transition communicates: "You have moved to a new location in the hierarchy."

Peak

Breadcrumbs display the complete new hierarchy. Current location is indicated.

Resolution

Breadcrumbs persist in their new state. Hover and click states available.

End State

Breadcrumbs showing current location. Hierarchy clear.

Accessibility

Breadcrumb updates must be announced to screen readers. Focus management if appropriate.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

Breadcrumbs update instantly. Hierarchy visible.

Review Questions

Do the breadcrumbs clearly show the current location?

Is the hierarchy communicated effectively?

Is the transition smooth and appropriate?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

SECTION 5: BUTTON RECIPES
Button animations communicate interactivity, provide feedback, and guide user action. Every button interaction tells a micro-story of cause and effect.

RECIPE: Hover
Purpose

Indicate that an element is interactive and ready for engagement. Hover feedback builds confidence and communicates affordance.

Trigger

Pointer enters the button's hit area.

Beginning

Button in standard state. No hover indication.

Transition

The button responds to pointer presence. Visual changes include subtle transformations—background color shift, elevation increase, scale growth, glow intensification. The response is immediate and proportional to the hover.

Peak

Button fully expresses its hover state. The user knows they can click.

Resolution

The hover state persists as long as the pointer remains. If the pointer leaves, the button returns to standard state.

End State

If pointer remains, hover state active. If pointer leaves, standard state.

Accessibility

Hover states must not be the only indication of interactivity. Focus states must provide equivalent feedback.

Performance

Simple property changes. GPU-friendly.

Failure State

No hover feedback. Users discover interactivity through click.

Review Questions

Does the hover state clearly indicate interactivity?

Is the feedback proportional to the interaction?

Is the transition smooth and immediate?

Are focus states equivalent?

RECIPE: Focus
Purpose

Indicate that an element is the current target for keyboard interaction. Focus feedback ensures accessibility and provides orientation for keyboard users.

Trigger

Keyboard navigation reaches the button. Tab key. Focus management.

Beginning

Button in standard state. No focus indication.

Transition

The button gains focus indicators. Typically a ring, outline, or highlight. The appearance is clear and distinct from hover states.

Peak

Focus state is fully visible. The user knows the button is ready for keyboard action.

Resolution

Focus state persists until focus moves elsewhere.

End State

Button focused. Ready for keyboard activation.

Accessibility

Focus indicators must be high contrast and clearly visible. Must not rely on color alone.

Performance

Simple property changes.

Failure State

No focus indication. Keyboard users cannot locate focus.

Review Questions

Is the focus indicator clearly visible?

Does it contrast with the hover state?

Is the focus state accessible and high contrast?

Are all interactive elements focusable?

RECIPE: Press
Purpose

Confirm that the user has initiated an action. Press feedback provides immediate tactile confirmation.

Trigger

Pointer down on button. Keyboard activation key down.

Beginning

Button in standard or hover state. No press indication.

Transition

The button responds to press with an immediate visual change—scale reduction, color darkening, elevation decrease. The response is instantaneous.

Peak

Button reaches its pressed state. The user feels the confirmation.

Resolution

The button returns to its previous state when the pointer or key is released—unless the press leads to a loading or success state.

End State

Button returns to standard state. Or transitions to next state.

Accessibility

Press feedback must work for keyboard and pointer interactions.

Performance

Instant response. No lag.

Failure State

No press feedback. Users may not know their click registered.

Review Questions

Is the press response immediate?

Does the press state provide clear confirmation?

Is the feedback consistent across interaction types?

Does the press lead appropriately to subsequent states?

RECIPE: Release
Purpose

Indicate that the user has completed the press action. Release feedback confirms that the button has been clicked.

Trigger

Pointer up on button. Keyboard activation key up.

Beginning

Button in pressed state. Action initiated.

Transition

Button releases from pressed state. The button may have a subtle bounce, settle, or transition to another state (loading, success, etc.).

Peak

Button fully releases from pressed state. The action is confirmed.

Resolution

Button moves to its next state based on the action result.

End State

Button in its standard state. Or loading. Or success.

Accessibility

Release feedback must be perceivable for all interaction types.

Performance

Simple transitions. No complex animations.

Failure State

Button jumps to next state without release feedback.

Review Questions

Does the release provide clear confirmation?

Is the transition appropriate to the action?

Does the release lead to the correct next state?

RECIPE: Success
Purpose

Communicate that an action has completed successfully. Success feedback provides closure and builds user confidence.

Trigger

Action completion. Button pressed and action successful.

Beginning

Button in standard or pressed state. Action in progress or complete.

Transition

Button transforms to a success state. Common patterns include a checkmark icon appearing, color shift to green, text change to "Done" or "Complete." The transition is smooth and positive.

Peak

Success state fully visible. The user knows their action succeeded.

Resolution

Success state persists briefly, then the button returns to standard state or remains in a success state until dismissed.

End State

Button in success state. Or returned to standard.

Accessibility

Success state must be announced to screen readers. Must not rely on color alone.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

Button returns to standard state. No success indication.

Review Questions

Does the success state provide clear confirmation?

Is the success communication appropriate to the action?

Does the success state persist appropriately?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Loading
Purpose

Communicate that an action is in progress and the system is working. Loading feedback manages expectations and prevents duplicate actions.

Trigger

Action initiated. Button pressed.

Beginning

Button in standard or pressed state. Action not yet complete.

Transition

Button transforms to a loading state. Label may change to "Processing" or similar. A spinner, progress indicator, or pulse animation appears. The transition communicates: "I'm working on it."

Peak

Loading state is fully visible. The user knows their action is processing.

Resolution

Loading state persists until the action completes. Then transitions to success, error, or standard state.

End State

Button in loading state. Or success. Or error. Or standard.

Accessibility

Loading state must be announced to screen readers. The button must be disabled or non-interactive during loading.

Performance

Smooth loading animation. Minimal CPU usage.

Failure State

Button remains standard. No loading indication.

Review Questions

Does the loading state clearly communicate progress?

Is the button disabled appropriately?

Does the loading animation match the action's duration?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Disabled
Purpose

Communicate that an action is not currently available. Disabled feedback sets clear expectations and prevents invalid interactions.

Trigger

State change makes action unavailable. Form incomplete. Conditions not met.

Beginning

Button in standard state. Action available.

Transition

Button transforms to disabled state. Visual changes include reduced opacity, grayed out appearance, and cursor change. The transition communicates: "This action isn't available right now."

Peak

Disabled state fully visible. The user cannot interact.

Resolution

Disabled state persists until conditions change. When conditions are met, button returns to standard state.

End State

Button disabled. Non-interactive.

Accessibility

Disabled state must be communicated to screen readers. The button must not be focusable or must be announced as disabled.

Performance

Simple property changes.

Failure State

Button remains interactive but non-functional. Confusing.

Review Questions

Does the disabled state clearly communicate unavailability?

Is the disabled state accessible to screen readers?

Does the button become interactive when conditions change?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

RECIPE: Magnetic Hover
Purpose

Create a dynamic, engaging hover experience that draws the user's attention and suggests interactivity. Magnetic hover adds a sense of physicality to the interface.

Trigger

Pointer approaches the button. Before entering the hit area.

Beginning

Button in standard state. Pointer not yet hovering.

Transition

As the pointer approaches, the button subtly moves toward the pointer. The effect is subtle—a few pixels at most. The motion creates a magnetic attraction.

Peak

The button is positioned closer to the pointer. The user feels the attraction.

Resolution

If the pointer enters, the button transitions to standard hover state. If the pointer leaves, the button returns to its standard position.

End State

Button in standard position. Or hover state.

Accessibility

Magnetic hover must not interfere with accessibility. Must not cause motion sickness. Reduced motion preferences must disable.

Performance

Simple transforms. Smooth tracking.

Failure State

No magnetic effect. Standard hover only.

Review Questions

Does the magnetic effect create engagement without distraction?

Is the effect subtle enough to avoid annoyance?

Does it respect reduced motion preferences?

Does it work alongside standard hover states?

RECIPE: Glow
Purpose

Emphasize a button's significance through luminous feedback. Glow states communicate importance, interactivity, and visual delight.

Trigger

Hover. Focus. Active state. Contextual emphasis.

Beginning

Button in standard state. No glow.

Transition

Button gains a glow effect—typically a halo, outer glow, or elevated shadow with color. The glow intensifies gradually.

Peak

Glow reaches full intensity. The button appears to radiate light.

Resolution

Glow persists during the state that triggered it. When the trigger ends, the glow fades.

End State

Button in standard state. No glow.

Accessibility

Glow must not cause photosensitive reactions. Must not rely on color alone.

Performance

Blur and shadow effects can be expensive. Use GPU acceleration.

Failure State

No glow. Standard states only.

Review Questions

Does the glow appropriately emphasize the button?

Is the glow effect smooth and polished?

Does the glow fade gracefully?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Ripple
Purpose

Provide tangible feedback for press interactions. Ripple effects confirm touch or click with a physical metaphor.

Trigger

Pointer down. Touch start. Keyboard activation.

Beginning

Button in standard state. No ripple effect.

Transition

A ripple radiates from the point of interaction. The ripple expands outward, fading as it grows. The effect communicates: "I felt that."

Peak

Ripple reaches its maximum size before fading.

Resolution

Ripple fades completely. Button returns to standard state or moves to next state.

End State

Button in standard state. No ripple visible.

Accessibility

Ripple must not cause photosensitive reactions. Must not be the only feedback.

Performance

Efficient. Can use canvas or CSS animations.

Failure State

No ripple. Standard press feedback only.

Review Questions

Does the ripple provide satisfying touch feedback?

Is the ripple effect smooth and responsive?

Does the ripple originate from the correct point?

Are performance constraints satisfied?

RECIPE: Primary CTA
Purpose

Distinguish the primary action from secondary options with appropriate visual weight and presence. Primary CTA animation emphasizes importance without alienating other actions.

Trigger

Page load. Section completion. State change.

Beginning

CTA in standard state. Present but not emphasized.

Transition

The CTA gains prominence. Size increases slightly. Color intensifies. Elevation rises. The transition communicates: "I'm the most important action here."

Peak

CTA reaches full emphasis. It commands attention without shouting.

Resolution

CTA persists in emphasized state. Hover and focus states remain functional.

End State

Primary CTA visually distinguished from other actions.

Accessibility

Emphasis must be perceivable without color. Provide text weight, size, or other indicators.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

CTA appears in standard state. All buttons equal.

Review Questions

Does the emphasis appropriately distinguish the primary action?

Is the emphasis achieved without overwhelming?

Are non-color indicators provided?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Secondary CTA
Purpose

Provide accessible secondary actions without competing with primary CTAs. Secondary CTA animation maintains hierarchy while ensuring usability.

Trigger

Page load. Section completion. State change.

Beginning

Secondary CTA in standard state. Present but neutral.

Transition

Secondary CTA gains subtle presence. Elevation may increase slightly. Color may shift toward the primary palette. The transition communicates: "I'm available when you need me."

Peak

Secondary CTA reaches its intended presence. Distinct from primary, clearly available.

Resolution

Secondary CTA persists in secondary state. Hover and focus states available.

End State

Secondary CTA present and usable. Subordinate to primary.

Accessibility

Must be fully interactive and focusable. Non-color indicators for state.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

Secondary CTA appears in standard state. Still functional.

Review Questions

Does the secondary CTA maintain appropriate hierarchy?

Is it clearly distinguishable from primary?

Is it fully interactive and accessible?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Danger Actions
Purpose

Communicate risk and require confirmation for destructive actions. Danger action animation signals caution and creates appropriate friction.

Trigger

Action that will delete, destroy, or irreversibly change data. Danger state.

Beginning

Action in standard state. No danger indication.

Transition

The action transforms to indicate danger. Color shifts to red or orange. Warning icons may appear. Text may change to reflect the risk. The transition communicates: "This action is destructive. Please be careful."

Peak

Danger state fully visible. The user understands the risk.

Resolution

Danger state persists until the action completes, is confirmed, or is cancelled. Secondary confirmation may be required.

End State

Action in danger state. Or cancelled. Or completed.

Accessibility

Danger state must be perceivable without color. Text must clearly communicate risk. Confirmation must be accessible.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

Action appears in standard state. Risk not communicated.

Review Questions

Does the danger state clearly communicate risk?

Is confirmation appropriately required for destructive actions?

Is the communication accessible without color?

Are accessibility requirements fully addressed?

SECTION 6: CARD RECIPES
Card animations provide structure, hierarchy, and visual organization for content. Cards are the atoms of interface composition.

RECIPE: Card Entrance
Purpose

Introduce card content with appropriate hierarchy and rhythm. Card entrance establishes the grid or list structure and guides the user's gaze.

Trigger

Page load. Section entry. Content update.

Beginning

Cards are present but diminished—opacity low, scale reduced, positions offset.

Transition

Cards enter with staggered timing. The stagger follows a logical pattern—left to right, top to bottom, or based on content importance. Each card settles into position with a small bounce or smooth stop.

Peak

All cards are visible. The grid or list is complete.

Resolution

Cards settle into their final positions. Hover and focus states ready.

End State

All cards visible. Grid or list complete.

Accessibility

Stagger timing must respect motion preferences. Screen readers must announce content.

Performance

Use GPU-accelerated transforms. Limit simultaneous animations.

Failure State

All cards appear instantly in final position.

Review Questions

Does the card entrance establish the grid or list structure?

Is the stagger pattern logical and purposeful?

Do cards settle gracefully?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Hover
Purpose

Indicate that a card is interactive and contains selectable content. Card hover builds engagement and suggests further exploration.

Trigger

Pointer enters card hit area.

Beginning

Card in standard state. No hover indication.

Transition

Card responds with subtle transformations—elevation increase, scale growth, glow intensification. Content within the card may also respond—images may zoom, text may emphasize.

Peak

Card fully expresses hover state. The user wants to click.

Resolution

Hover state persists while pointer remains. Returns to standard on exit.

End State

Card in hover state. Or standard.

Accessibility

Focus states must provide equivalent feedback.

Performance

Simple transforms. Avoid complex nested animations.

Failure State

No hover feedback.

Review Questions

Does hover indicate interactivity?

Is the feedback proportional and smooth?

Do focus states match hover?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Tilt
Purpose

Add depth and physicality to card interactions. Tilt effects create a sense of 3D presence and tactile engagement.

Trigger

Pointer movement over card. Hover state.

Beginning

Card flat. No tilt.

Transition

The card tilts based on pointer position relative to its center. The tilt is subtle—a few degrees in any direction. The effect creates the illusion of a physical object responding to touch.

Peak

Card reaches maximum tilt angle. The user feels the 3D presence.

Resolution

Tilt follows pointer movement continuously. On pointer exit, tilt returns to flat.

End State

Card flat. Or tilted based on pointer position.

Accessibility

Reduced motion preferences must disable tilt. Must not cause motion sickness.

Performance

Simple transforms. Use GPU acceleration.

Failure State

No tilt. Standard hover only.

Review Questions

Does the tilt effect create satisfying physicality?

Is the tilt subtle enough to avoid distraction?

Does it respect reduced motion preferences?

Does it work alongside other interactions?

RECIPE: Depth
Purpose

Communicate card hierarchy and importance through depth cues. Depth animation establishes visual layering and content priority.

Trigger

Page load. State change. Content update.

Beginning

Cards at their base depth position. No elevation.

Transition

Cards gain or lose elevation based on their importance. Primary cards rise higher. Secondary cards remain lower. The transition communicates hierarchy.

Peak

Depth hierarchy fully established. Cards arranged in intended layers.

Resolution

Depth persists until state change. Cards maintain their positions.

End State

Cards at intended depth levels. Hierarchy clear.

Accessibility

Depth must be perceivable without shadow or elevation cues.

Performance

Shadow rendering can be expensive. Use simple techniques.

Failure State

All cards at same depth. Hierarchy less clear.

Review Questions

Does depth effectively communicate hierarchy?

Is the depth difference sufficient to be perceivable?

Are non-shadow cues provided?

Are performance constraints satisfied?

RECIPE: Expansion
Purpose

Reveal additional card content with clarity and hierarchy. Card expansion provides progressive disclosure without navigation.

Trigger

Card click. Expand trigger. State change.

Beginning

Card collapsed. Only summary content visible.

Transition

Card expands to reveal additional content. The expansion is graceful—content slides, fades, or scales into view. The transition communicates: "There's more to see here."

Peak

Card fully expanded. All content visible.

Resolution

Expanded card persists until collapse trigger. Other cards may shift to accommodate.

End State

Card expanded. Additional content visible.

Accessibility

Focus management must be complete. Expanded content must be accessible. Screen readers must announce expansion.

Performance

Content should be rendered before animation to avoid layout thrashing.

Failure State

Card appears expanded instantly. Content visible.

Review Questions

Does the expansion clearly reveal additional content?

Is the transition smooth and appropriate?

Are focus and accessibility requirements satisfied?

Do other cards accommodate the expansion gracefully?

RECIPE: Collapse
Purpose

Return expanded card to its compact state. Collapse provides closure and restores the grid or list structure.

Trigger

Collapse trigger. Click. State change.

Beginning

Card expanded. Full content visible.

Transition

Card collapses gracefully. Content slides, fades, or scales out of view. The transition communicates: "I'm returning to summary view."

Peak

Card returns to compact state. Only summary visible.

Resolution

Card settles into its compact position. Other cards may shift to fill space.

End State

Card collapsed. Summary visible.

Accessibility

Focus management must be complete. Screen readers must announce collapse.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

Card appears collapsed instantly.

Review Questions

Does the collapse gracefully return to summary view?

Is the transition appropriate and smooth?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

Do other cards accommodate the collapse gracefully?

RECIPE: Selection
Purpose

Indicate that a card has been selected or activated. Selection provides confirmation and orientation.

Trigger

Card click. Selection state change. Keyboard activation.

Beginning

Card in standard state. Not selected.

Transition

Card transforms to selected state. Visual changes include border highlight, checkmark, background shift, or elevation change. The transition is positive and confirming.

Peak

Selected state fully visible. The user knows the card is selected.

Resolution

Selection persists until deselected or state changes.

End State

Card selected. Clearly distinguished.

Accessibility

Selection must be perceivable without color. Include icon, text, or other indicators.

Performance

Simple transforms.

Failure State

No selection indication.

Review Questions

Is the selected state clearly distinguishable?

Is the transition positive and confirming?

Are non-color indicators included?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Focus
Purpose

Indicate keyboard focus on a card. Focus feedback ensures card accessibility for keyboard users.

Trigger

Keyboard navigation reaches card. Focus management.

Beginning

Card in standard state. No focus.

Transition

Card gains focus indicators. Typically a ring, outline, or highlight.

Peak

Focus state fully visible. Keyboard user knows they're on the card.

Resolution

Focus persists until focus moves.

End State

Card focused.

Accessibility

Focus indicators must be high contrast. Must not rely on color alone.

Performance

Simple property changes.

Failure State

No focus indication.

Review Questions

Is the focus indicator clearly visible?

Does it provide equivalent feedback to hover?

Are all interactive cards focusable?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Grouping
Purpose

Communicate relationships between cards through grouping animations. Grouping establishes visual hierarchy and content organization.

Trigger

Page load. Content organization. State change.

Beginning

Cards appear individually. No grouping apparent.

Transition

Cards coalesce into groups. The group may gain a container, background, or spacing change. Group members may animate together.

Peak

Groups fully established. Relationships clear.

Resolution

Groups persist until reorganization.

End State

Cards arranged in groups. Relationships clear.

Accessibility

Grouping must be perceivable without visual cues. Include headings, labels, or other indicators.

Performance

Simple transitions. Avoid complex group animations.

Failure State

Cards appear without grouping.

Review Questions

Do groups effectively communicate relationships?

Are groupings perceivable without visual cues?

Is the grouping organization logical and useful?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Comparison
Purpose

Enable side-by-side comparison of card content. Comparison animations establish relationships and highlight differences.

Trigger

Comparison mode activated. Selection state.

Beginning

Cards in standard layout. No comparison state.

Transition

Cards reposition for comparison. Adjacent cards may highlight, shift, or gain comparison indicators. The transition communicates: "Let's compare these."

Peak

Comparison state fully established. Cards ready for comparison.

Resolution

Comparison persists until dismissed or completed.

End State

Cards in comparison arrangement.

Accessibility

Comparison state must be announced to screen readers.

Performance

Simple transforms.

Failure State

Cards remain in standard layout.

Review Questions

Does the comparison arrangement enable effective comparison?

Is the transition clear and purposeful?

Is the comparison state accessible?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Feature Cards
Purpose

Present feature content with appropriate emphasis and hierarchy. Feature card animations establish importance and guide attention.

Trigger

Page load. Section entry. Feature presentation.

Beginning

Feature cards in standard state. Some emphasis possible.

Transition

Feature cards gain appropriate emphasis based on their importance. Primary features may be larger, more elevated, or more animated. Secondary features more subtle.

Peak

Feature hierarchy fully established.

Resolution

Feature cards persist with appropriate emphasis.

End State

Feature cards at intended emphasis levels.

Accessibility

Emphasis must be perceivable without color or motion.

Performance

Simple transitions.

Failure State

All features equal.

Review Questions

Does the feature presentation establish clear hierarchy?

Is the emphasis appropriate for each feature?

Are non-visual cues provided?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Project Cards
Purpose

Present project content with structure and visual richness. Project card animations establish portfolio or project hierarchy.

Trigger

Page load. Portfolio entry. Project view.

Beginning

Project cards in standard state. Some emphasis possible.

Transition

Project cards gain appropriate presentation. Images may reveal, tags may animate, descriptions may appear. The transition communicates: "Here are our projects."

Peak

Project portfolio fully visible. Projects organized and accessible.

Resolution

Projects persist with appropriate structure.

End State

Project cards fully visible. Portfolio complete.

Accessibility

Project content must be accessible to screen readers. Focus management complete.

Performance

Image reveals should be smooth. Avoid layout thrashing.

Failure State

Projects appear in standard list.

Review Questions

Does the project presentation effectively showcase work?

Is the visual hierarchy appropriate?

Are projects easily browsable?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Architecture Cards
Purpose

Present architectural concepts with visual clarity and technical credibility. Architecture card animations communicate structure and relationships.

Trigger

Architecture page load. Technical presentation.

Beginning

Architecture cards in standard state. Technical content waiting.

Transition

Cards reveal technical detail with appropriate pacing. Diagrams or icons may animate. Relationships between cards may be indicated through connected animations.

Peak

Architecture fully visible. Technical content accessible.

Resolution

Architecture cards persist with appropriate technical emphasis.

End State

Technical cards visible. Architecture clear.

Accessibility

Technical content must be accessible. Diagrams must have text alternatives.

Performance

Complex diagram animations may be heavy. Optimize.

Failure State

Architecture content appears in standard format.

Review Questions

Does the architecture presentation communicate technical content effectively?

Are relationships between cards clear?

Is the content accessible to all users?

Are performance constraints satisfied?

SECTION 7: WINDOW RECIPES
Window animations create the spatial framework for the Zenuxs interface. They establish the boundaries, relationships, and interactions of content containers.

RECIPE: Open
Purpose

Reveal a window with appropriate ceremony and spatial context. Window open establishes the new content container within the interface.

Trigger

Window creation. Content activation.

Beginning

Window invisible or not present. Content not available.

Transition

Window appears. The appearance can be from the center, from the edge, or with a specific spatial origin. Window gains its position, size, and visual state. Content within reveals appropriately.

Peak

Window fully open. Content visible.

Resolution

Window settles into its position. Interactive elements ready.

End State

Window open. Content accessible.

Accessibility

Focus must move appropriately. Screen readers must announce new window.

Performance

Smooth transforms. Avoid layout thrashing.

Failure State

Window appears instantly. Content visible.

Review Questions

Does the window open establish spatial context?

Is the appearance appropriate for the window's purpose?

Is focus management complete?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Close
Purpose

Dismiss a window cleanly and prepare for next state. Window close provides closure and frees spatial resources.

Trigger

Window dismissal. Content deactivation.

Beginning

Window open. Content visible.

Transition

Window closes. The closure can be the reverse of the open animation, a fade-out, or a specific dismissal pattern. Content may fade or collapse.

Peak

Window nearly closed. Only minimal presence remains.

Resolution

Window disappears completely. Underlying interface visible.

End State

Window closed. Content inaccessible. Interface ready.

Accessibility

Focus must move to appropriate location. Screen readers must announce window close.

Performance

Simple transforms.

Failure State

Window disappears instantly.

Review Questions

Does the window close provide appropriate closure?

Is the dismissal pattern consistent with the open pattern?

Is focus returned appropriately?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Expand
Purpose

Increase window size to reveal additional content or provide more space. Window expansion enables content focus and detail viewing.

Trigger

Expand trigger. Maximize button. Double-click title bar.

Beginning

Window at standard size. Content visible but potentially constrained.

Transition

Window expands gracefully. Edges move outward. Content adjusts. The transition is smooth and maintains orientation.

Peak

Window at expanded size. More content visible.

Resolution

Window persists at expanded size until restored or collapsed.

End State

Window expanded. More content accessible.

Accessibility

Content changes must be announced if significant. Focus should remain usable.

Performance

Smooth resizing. Avoid content reflow during animation.

Failure State

Window jumps to expanded size instantly.

Review Questions

Does the expansion provide useful additional space?

Is the expansion smooth and maintain orientation?

Is content appropriately adjusted?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Collapse
Purpose

Reduce window size to save space while preserving access. Window collapse provides efficient space management.

Trigger

Collapse trigger. Minimize button. Double-click title bar.

Beginning

Window at standard or expanded size.

Transition

Window collapses gracefully. Edges move inward. Content may condense or hide. The transition maintains the window's identity while reducing its footprint.

Peak

Window at collapsed size. Minimal content visible.

Resolution

Window persists at collapsed size until expanded.

End State

Window collapsed. Space saved. Content accessible via expansion.

Accessibility

Window state must be announced if significant.

Performance

Smooth resizing.

Failure State

Window jumps to collapsed size instantly.

Review Questions

Does the collapse provide efficient space use?

Is the collapse smooth and maintain identity?

Is the collapsed state still accessible?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Minimize
Purpose

Temporarily hide a window while preserving its state. Window minimize provides clutter reduction while maintaining context.

Trigger

Minimize button. Keyboard shortcut. Gesture.

Beginning

Window open. Content visible.

Transition

Window reduces to a minimized representation. The transition can be a scale to icon size, a slide to a dock, or a fade to a taskbar position. The transition communicates: "I'm still here, just smaller."

Peak

Window minimized. Representation visible in dock, taskbar, or other location.

Resolution

Window persists minimized until restored.

End State

Window minimized. Representation visible.

Accessibility

Window state must be announced. Focus must move appropriately.

Performance

Smooth transforms.

Failure State

Window disappears instantly. No representation.

Review Questions

Does the minimize provide effective clutter reduction?

Is the minimized representation clear and accessible?

Is the window's state preserved correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Restore
Purpose

Return a minimized window to its previous state. Restore provides access to hidden content.

Trigger

Restore trigger. Click on minimized representation. Keyboard shortcut.

Beginning

Window minimized. Representation visible.

Transition

Window restores to its previous size and position. The transition is the reverse of the minimize animation.

Peak

Window at full size. Content visible.

Resolution

Window settles into its restored state. Content accessible.

End State

Window restored. Content accessible.

Accessibility

Focus must move to the window. Screen readers must announce restoration.

Performance

Smooth transforms.

Failure State

Window appears at full size instantly.

Review Questions

Does the restore provide effective access to content?

Is the restore consistent with the minimize pattern?

Is focus managed correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Focus
Purpose

Indicate that a window has focus and is the current interaction target. Window focus establishes hierarchy and orientation.

Trigger

Window click. Window activation. Keyboard focus.

Beginning

Window in background state. Diminished visual presence.

Transition

Window gains focus indicators—brightness increase, elevation rise, title bar emphasis. The transition communicates: "This window is now active."

Peak

Focus state fully established. Window clearly active.

Resolution

Focus persists until another window receives focus.

End State

Window focused. Active and interactive.

Accessibility

Focus must be communicated to screen readers.

Performance

Simple property changes.

Failure State

No focus indication. All windows appear equal.

Review Questions

Is the focus state clearly distinguishable?

Does the focus transition feel responsive?

Is focus communicated to screen readers?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Blur
Purpose

Indicate that a window has lost focus and is now in the background. Window blur communicates hierarchy and minimizes distraction.

Trigger

Another window receives focus. Background click.

Beginning

Window focused. Active and prominent.

Transition

Window loses focus indicators—brightness decreases, elevation drops, title bar deemphasizes. The transition communicates: "This window is now in the background."

Peak

Blur state fully established. Window in background.

Resolution

Blur persists until window receives focus again.

End State

Window blurred. Background. Less prominent.

Accessibility

Blur state must be communicable to screen readers.

Performance

Simple property changes.

Failure State

No blur indication. All windows appear equal.

Review Questions

Is the blur state clearly distinguishable?

Does the blur transition feel appropriate?

Is blur communicated to screen readers?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Window Switching
Purpose

Transition between active windows with orientation and continuity. Window switching provides navigation between content contexts.

Trigger

Window selection. Keyboard shortcut (Alt+Tab or similar). Taskbar click.

Beginning

Current window active. Target window in background.

Transition

Windows swap their focus and prominence states. The transition can be a cross-fade, a spatial animation, or a slide.

Peak

Target window becomes active. Previous window becomes background.

Resolution

New window focused. Interface stable.

End State

Target window active. Previous window background.

Accessibility

Window switching must be announced. Focus must move correctly.

Performance

Smooth transitions.

Failure State

Windows switch instantly. No transition.

Review Questions

Does the window switch maintain orientation?

Is the transition smooth and purposeful?

Is focus managed correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Window Stacking
Purpose

Manage the spatial arrangement of overlapping windows. Window stacking establishes depth hierarchy and visual organization.

Trigger

Window creation. Window activation. Spatial arrangement.

Beginning

Windows arranged. Stacking not optimized.

Transition

Windows reorder in the Z-axis. Active windows rise. Background windows descend. The transition communicates hierarchy.

Peak

Stacking hierarchy fully established.

Resolution

Stacking persists until windows change.

End State

Windows in correct stacking order. Hierarchy clear.

Accessibility

Stacking must be navigable by keyboard. Focus management complete.

Performance

Simple property changes.

Failure State

All windows at same Z-level.

Review Questions

Does the stacking effectively communicate hierarchy?

Is the stacking order logical and predictable?

Is the stacking navigable by keyboard?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Floating Panels
Purpose

Provide movable, persistent panels for tools and information. Floating panel animations establish accessibility and spatial flexibility.

Trigger

Panel creation. Panel toggle. Spatial movement.

Beginning

Panel in standard position. Or hidden.

Transition

Panel moves or appears. The transition is smooth and maintains the panel's relationship to the interface.

Peak

Panel in its position. Content accessible.

Resolution

Panel persists until moved or closed.

End State

Panel in position. Tool or information accessible.

Accessibility

Panels must be movable by keyboard. Focus management complete.

Performance

Smooth transforms.

Failure State

Panel appears in default position.

Review Questions

Does the panel position provide effective tool access?

Is the panel easily movable and accessible?

Is the panel's content readily accessible?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Workspace Layout
Purpose

Organize windows into an effective workspace arrangement. Workspace layout establishes spatial efficiency and workflow optimization.

Trigger

Workspace switch. Layout selection. State change.

Beginning

Windows in previous layout. Not optimized for current task.

Transition

Windows reposition to new layout. The transition is coordinated—windows move together to their new positions.

Peak

New layout fully established. Windows in optimal positions.

Resolution

Layout persists until workspace changes.

End State

Windows arranged in workspace layout.

Accessibility

Focus must move to appropriate window. Screen readers must announce layout change.

Performance

Coordinated animations. Maintain smoothness.

Failure State

Windows appear in new layout instantly.

Review Questions

Does the workspace layout optimize workflow?

Is the transition coordinated and smooth?

Is focus managed correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

SECTION 8: CLI RECIPES
CLI animations create the authentic, responsive, and engaging command-line experience that defines Zenuxs.

RECIPE: Typing
Purpose

Reveal command output or code with appropriate pacing and authenticity. Typing animation establishes credibility and engagement.

Trigger

Command execution. Output generation. Content reveal.

Beginning

No visible content. Awaiting output.

Transition

Characters appear sequentially. The pace is authentic—faster than human typing but not instant. The rhythm can vary based on character type.

Peak

Output complete. Full text visible.

Resolution

Text settles. Cursor moves to next position.

End State

Full command output visible. Ready for next input.

Accessibility

Screen readers must have access to complete output. Timing must be adjustable.

Performance

Efficient. Character animations should be smooth.

Failure State

Full output appears instantly.

Review Questions

Does the typing pacing feel authentic?

Is the rhythm appropriate for the content?

Does the typing create engagement without impatience?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Cursor
Purpose

Indicate where input will appear and maintain continuity. Cursor animation provides orientation and a sense of readiness.

Trigger

Terminal active. Prompt ready.

Beginning

Cursor visible. Ready for input.

Transition

Cursor blinks with a consistent rhythm. The blink rate is comfortable—not too fast, not too slow.

Peak

Cursor at its most visible point.

Resolution

Cursor continues its rhythm. Ready for input.

End State

Cursor active. Blinking. Ready.

Accessibility

Cursor must be high contrast and clearly visible. Screen readers must announce cursor state.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

Cursor static. Still visible.

Review Questions

Is the cursor clearly visible and high contrast?

Is the blink rhythm comfortable?

Does the cursor indicate readiness for input?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Streaming
Purpose

Reveal content continuously, simulating real-time generation or processing. Streaming creates authenticity and engagement.

Trigger

AI generation. Data processing. Real-time output.

Beginning

No content visible. Processing or generating.

Transition

Content appears continuously—character by character, word by word, or line by line. The pace varies based on content type.

Peak

Content fully streamed. Output complete.

Resolution

Streaming complete. Content ready.

End State

Full content visible. Ready for next step.

Accessibility

Screen readers must have access to streamed content. Users must be able to pause or control stream pace.

Performance

Efficient. Stream must not block other interactions.

Failure State

Full content appears instantly.

Review Questions

Does streaming create appropriate authenticity?

Is the stream pace comfortable and appropriate?

Can users control or pause the stream?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Reasoning
Purpose

Communicate that the system is processing and thinking. Reasoning animation sets appropriate expectations.

Trigger

AI processing. System calculation. Data analysis.

Beginning

System idle. Awaiting input or processing.

Transition

A reasoning indicator appears. Common patterns include a thinking animation, a progress indicator, or a processing state.

Peak

Reasoning fully indicated. User expects results.

Resolution

Reasoning transitions to response or outcome.

End State

Reasoning complete. Result visible.

Accessibility

Reasoning state must be announced to screen readers.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

No reasoning indication. Results appear abruptly.

Review Questions

Does reasoning set appropriate expectations?

Is the reasoning indication clear and communicative?

Does it transition properly to results?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Progress
Purpose

Show completion status for longer operations. Progress provides orientation and expectation management.

Trigger

Long-running operation. Processing with duration.

Beginning

Progress indicator appears. Operation starting.

Transition

Progress advances continuously or in steps. The indicator updates to reflect completion.

Peak

Progress reaches completion. Operation finishing.

Resolution

Progress transitions to success or completion.

End State

Progress complete. Operation done.

Accessibility

Progress must be announced to screen readers. Percentage if available.

Performance

Efficient updates.

Failure State

No progress indication. Operation opaque.

Review Questions

Does progress provide useful orientation?

Is the progress tracking accurate and smooth?

Is progress communicated to screen readers?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Execution
Purpose

Communicate that a command is running. Execution animation provides feedback and sets expectations.

Trigger

Command submitted. Action initiated.

Beginning

Command visible. Awaiting response.

Transition

Execution indicator appears. Animation suggests processing.

Peak

Execution fully indicated. Operation running.

Resolution

Execution completes. Command output appears.

End State

Execution complete. Output visible.

Accessibility

Execution state must be announced.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

No execution indication. Results appear or don't.

Review Questions

Does execution provide clear feedback?

Is the execution indication appropriate?

Does it transition properly to output?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Success
Purpose

Confirm successful command execution. Success feedback provides closure and builds confidence.

Trigger

Command succeeds. Operation completes.

Beginning

Execution state. Awaiting completion.

Transition

Success indicator appears—checkmark, green text, or positive message. The transition communicates: "Success."

Peak

Success fully visible. User knows operation succeeded.

Resolution

Success persists briefly. Then returns to prompt.

End State

Success visible. Prompt ready for next command.

Accessibility

Success must be announced to screen readers.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

No success indication. User uncertain.

Review Questions

Does success provide clear confirmation?

Is the success communication positive and clear?

Does success persist appropriately?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Failure
Purpose

Communicate that a command or operation failed. Failure feedback manages expectations and guides recovery.

Trigger

Command fails. Operation error.

Beginning

Execution state. Operation failing.

Transition

Failure indicator appears—error message, red text, or warning. The transition communicates: "An error occurred."

Peak

Failure fully visible. User knows operation failed.

Resolution

Failure persists. User can try again or fix issue.

End State

Failure visible. Error message accessible. Prompt ready.

Accessibility

Failure must be announced. Error messages must be clear.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

No failure indication. User confused.

Review Questions

Does failure provide clear communication?

Are error messages clear and actionable?

Is failure announced to screen readers?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Retry
Purpose

Provide a path to recover from failure. Retry animation communicates resilience and guidance.

Trigger

Command failure. Retry available.

Beginning

Failure visible. Retry option not yet indicated.

Transition

Retry option appears. Can be automatic or user-triggered.

Peak

Retry available. User can try again.

Resolution

Retry starts operation again.

End State

Operation retrying. Or command ready.

Accessibility

Retry must be keyboard accessible. Must be announced.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

No retry option. User must start over.

Review Questions

Does retry provide a clear recovery path?

Is the retry accessible and discoverable?

Does retry start operation correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Cancellation
Purpose

Allow users to abort operations. Cancellation provides control and reduces frustration.

Trigger

User cancellation. Escape key. Cancel trigger.

Beginning

Operation running. Cancellation available.

Transition

Operation stops. Cancellation indicator appears.

Peak

Operation cancelled. User knows.

Resolution

Operation fully stopped. Interface ready.

End State

Command ready. Operation cancelled.

Accessibility

Cancellation must be keyboard accessible. Must be announced.

Performance

Immediate.

Failure State

No cancellation. User stuck.

Review Questions

Does cancellation provide control?

Is cancellation accessible and discoverable?

Does cancellation stop operation correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Command History
Purpose

Reveal past commands for reference and reuse. Command history provides continuity and efficiency.

Trigger

History request. Up arrow. History view.

Beginning

Prompt visible. No history shown.

Transition

History entries appear. Navigation is possible.

Peak

History fully visible. Commands accessible.

Resolution

User selects a command. History returns.

End State

Selected command entered. Or history dismissed.

Accessibility

History must be keyboard navigable. Screen readers must announce.

Performance

Efficient.

Failure State

No history visible.

Review Questions

Does history provide useful command access?

Is history navigable and accessible?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Command Suggestions
Purpose

Prompt visible. User typing.

Transition

Suggestions appear. Relevant completions shown.

Peak

Suggestions visible. User can select.

Command entered. Or suggestions dismissed.

Efficient.

Failure State

Are suggestions accessible and selectable?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

Transition

Command fully completed. Or options visible.

Command entered. Or completion accepted.

Failure State

SECTION 9: TERMINAL RECIPES
Terminal animations create the authentic, immersive command-line environment that defines the Zenuxs CLI experience.

RECIPE: Boot
Purpose

Initialize the terminal environment with ceremony and authenticity. Terminal boot establishes the CLI as a living system.

Trigger

Terminal launch. CLI start.

Beginning

Dark terminal. Cursor pulse only.

Transition

Boot messages appear. Lines of system output stream in. Kernel information, system checks, environment setup. The output is authentic and meaningful.

Peak

Boot complete. Prompt visible.

Resolution

Terminal ready. Prompt awaits input.

End State

Prompt visible. Terminal active.

Accessibility

Boot messages must be accessible to screen readers.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

Prompt appears instantly. No boot ceremony.

Review Questions

Does boot establish the terminal as authentic?

Are boot messages meaningful and credible?

Does boot create anticipation?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

RECIPE: Prompt
Purpose

Trigger

Beginning

Transition

Peak

Prompt visible. Ready.

Resolution

Prompt persists. Ready.

End State

Prompt visible. Cursor active.

Accessibility

Prompt must be announced to screen readers.

Performance

Minimal.

Failure State

No prompt. Users uncertain.

Review Questions

Is prompt visible and accessible?

Does prompt communicate state correctly?

Are accessibility requirements satisfied?

SECTION 14: SCROLL RECIPES
Scroll animations create narrative, depth, and engagement through the user's movement through content.

RECIPE: Pinned Sections
Purpose

Create sections that remain visible while content changes. Pinned sections provide structure and narrative.

Trigger

Scroll reaches section.

Beginning

Transition

New section pins. Content transitions.

Peak

Section fully pinned.

Resolution

Section remains pinned until unpinned.

End State

Section pinned. Content visible.

Accessibility

Scroll interactions must be keyboard accessible.

Performance

Efficient.

Failure State

Sections scroll normally.

SECTION 22: ANIMATION COMBINATIONS
Recipes combine to create complete experiences. Understanding combination principles ensures harmony.

How Recipes Combine
Recipes are not isolated. They exist in systems. Every animation exists alongside others. The combination must be harmonious, not chaotic.

Principles of Combination:

Recipes must share underlying timing and pacing characteristics. A recipe with fast pacing next to a recipe with slow pacing creates rhythm disruption. Establish a tempo and maintain it.

Recipes must respect hierarchy. Primary animations may dominate. Secondary animations must support. No animation should compete for attention against more important animations.

Recipes must coordinate their beginnings and endings. A new animation should not begin before a previous animation resolves—unless orchestrated for effect.

Recipes must respect spatial relationships. Adjacent animations should not move in conflicting directions or with conflicting energy.

Timing Orchestration
Stagger Timing:

Elements enter in sequence, not simultaneously. Stagger creates rhythm and guides attention.

Choreographed Timing:

Multiple animations coordinate—this moves while that moves, but in complementary ways.

Hierarchical Timing:

Primary content animates first. Secondary content follows. Supporting content animates last.

State-Based Timing:

Different states have different timing profiles. Loading is slow and patient. Success is quick and celebratory. Error is immediate and clear.

Harmony Maintenance
Recipes maintain harmony through:

Shared Easing: All animations use the same easing curves. No animation feels like it belongs to a different system.

Shared Duration Bands: Animations fall into duration bands. Micro-interactions share micro durations. Hero animations share hero durations.

Shared Spatial Logic: Elements that relate spatially move in related ways. Adjacent elements move together or in complementary patterns.

Shared Intensity Levels: Intensity is consistent. No animation is dramatically more or less energetic than its peers.

Scaling Animation Systems
As the system grows, combination patterns become more complex. Maintain discipline:

Animation Tiers: Define tiers of animation intensity. Tier 1: Critical, highly visible. Tier 2: Important but secondary. Tier 3: Supportive, ambient. Ensure combinations respect tiers.

Animation Registers: Define registers of animation—heroic, functional, ambient, micro. Ensure combinations mix registers appropriately.

Animation Density: Define maximum animation density. No more than X elements should animate simultaneously. Always maintain rest areas.

SECTION 23: RECIPE SELECTION FRAMEWORK
Contributors and AI agents need a clear framework for selecting the appropriate recipe for any given animation need.

How Contributors Choose Recipes
Step 1: Identify the Purpose

What must this animation communicate? Start with purpose, not effect.

Step 2: Identify the Trigger

What causes this animation? User action? System event? State change?

Step 3: Identify the Category

Which recipe category does this animation belong to? Hero? Navigation? AI? Architecture?

Step 4: Select the Specific Recipe

Within the category, choose the recipe that best matches the purpose, trigger, and context.

Step 5: Apply the Recipe

Follow the recipe exactly. Do not improvise without approval.

Step 6: Validate

Use the recipe's Review Questions to ensure correct application.

How AI Chooses Recipes
AI agents follow the same framework. Additionally:

AI must consider the recipe's Accessibility requirements before all other criteria.

AI must prioritize recipes that match the category of the content being animated.

AI must default to simpler recipes when uncertain.

AI must document its selection rationale.

How Recipes Evolve
Recipes evolve through:

Annual Review: All recipes reviewed annually for relevance and effectiveness.

Usage Analysis: Recipes that are rarely used are candidates for deprecation.

Performance Analysis: Recipes with consistent performance issues are candidates for revision.

Accessibility Analysis: Recipes that fail accessibility requirements are immediately reviewed.

Feedback Collection: Designers, engineers, and users provide feedback on recipe effectiveness.

When Recipes Merge
Recipes merge when they are essentially identical in purpose and execution. Merged recipes maintain the most complete version.

When Recipes Split
Recipes split when a single recipe is applied to meaningfully different contexts. Splitting creates more specific recipes for more specific needs.

When Recipes Retire
Recipes retire when:

No longer used in the codebase

Replaced by more effective recipes

Incompatible with accessibility requirements

Incompatible with performance requirements

Superseded by better approaches

Retirement requires a deprecation period, replacement documentation, and migration support.

SECTION 24: RECIPE ANTI-PATTERNS
Anti-patterns are the enemies of good animation. Avoid them absolutely.

1. Random Motion
What it is: Motion without purpose. Elements move because they can, not because they should.

Why it hurts: Random motion is noise, not communication. It distracts, confuses, and undermines the interface.

How to avoid it: Every animation must answer "What does this motion communicate?" If the answer is unclear, don't animate.

2. Repeated Recipes
What it is: The same recipe applied too frequently, losing impact.

Why it hurts: Repetition reduces significance. When everything is special, nothing is.

How to avoid it: Reserve high-impact recipes for high-impact moments.

3. Animation Overload
What it is: Too many elements animating simultaneously.

Why it hurts: Overload overwhelms users, increases cognitive load, and creates visual chaos.

How to avoid it: Maintain animation density limits. Ensure rest areas exist.

4. Competing Animations
What it is: Multiple animations vying for the same attention.

Why it hurts: Users cannot process multiple focal points simultaneously. Competing animations cancel each other's effectiveness.

How to avoid it: Establish clear hierarchy. One dominant animation per moment.

5. Overlapping Timelines
What it is: Animations that begin before previous animations have resolved.

Why it hurts: Overlaps create visual clutter and prevent closure.

How to avoid it: Allow animations to complete before starting new ones—unless specifically orchestrated.

6. Meaningless Transitions
What it is: Transitions that add no value, just motion for motion's sake.

Why it hurts: Wasted motion wastes user attention and processing.

How to avoid it: Every transition should communicate a clear state change or spatial relationship.

7. Weak Endings
What it is: Animations that fizzle out rather than resolve.

Why it hurts: Weak endings undermine narrative structure and leave users unsatisfied.

How to avoid it: Every animation must have a clear resolution and end state.

8. No Resolution
What it is: Animations that never complete.

Why it hurts: Perpetual motion is distracting and implies the system is never ready.

How to avoid it: Every animation must reach a stable end state.

9. Broken Rhythm
What it is: Inconsistent timing, pacing, or intensity.

Why it hurts: Broken rhythm creates disorientation and prevents users from anticipating motion.

How to avoid it: Maintain consistent pacing across related animations.

10. Performance Ignorance
What it is: Animations that ignore performance constraints.

Why it hurts: Janky, stuttering animations destroy user experience.

How to avoid it: Always optimize. Always target 60fps minimum.

11. Accessibility Negligence
What it is: Animations that ignore reduced motion, photosensitivity, or screen reader compatibility.

Why it hurts: Users with different needs cannot use the system fully.

How to avoid it: Accessibility must be considered before all other criteria.

12. Over-Orchestration
What it is: Trying to control every aspect of motion.

Why it hurts: Over-orchestration creates rigidity and prevents natural responsiveness.

How to avoid it: Allow some motion to be emergent and responsive.

13. Under-Orchestration
What it is: No coordination between animations.

Why it hurts: Chaos. Disconnected motion.

How to avoid it: Coordinate animations that share space or context.

14. Easing Ignorance
What it is: Ignoring easing curves.

Why it hurts: Un-eased motion feels mechanical and unnatural.

How to avoid it: Always use appropriate easing.

15. Duration Mismatch
What it is: Inconsistent durations.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent duration disrupts rhythm and predictability.

How to avoid it: Use consistent duration bands.

16. Hierarchy Violation
What it is: Secondary animations that overshadow primary.

Why it hurts: Undermines visual hierarchy and communication.

How to avoid it: Respect hierarchy in all animations.

17. Spatial Inconsistency
What it is: Conflicting spatial logic.

Why it hurts: Creates disorientation.

How to avoid it: Maintain spatial consistency.

18. Emotional Mismatch
What it is: Animation that doesn't match emotional context.

Why it hurts: Mismatch creates cognitive dissonance.

How to avoid it: Align animation with emotional goal.

19. Timeline Complexity
What it is: Overly complex animation timelines.

Why it hurts: Hard to maintain, hard to understand.

How to avoid it: Keep timelines simple and clear.

20. User Control Ignorance
What it is: Animations that ignore user control preferences.

Why it hurts: Reduces accessibility and user satisfaction.

How to avoid it: Always respect user preferences.

21. State Confusion
What it is: Animations that don't communicate state clearly.

Why it hurts: Users uncertain about system state.

How to avoid it: Every animation should clarify state.

22. Delayed Feedback
What it is: Slow response to user actions.

Why it hurts: Reduces perceived responsiveness.

How to avoid it: Immediate feedback is essential.

23. Excessive Duration
What it is: Animations that take too long.

Why it hurts: Impatience and frustration.

How to avoid it: Keep durations appropriate.

24. Insufficient Duration
What it is: Animations that are too fast.

Why it hurts: Users miss the animation entirely.

How to avoid it: Ensure animations are perceivable.

25. Motion Blindness
What it is: Animations that go unnoticed.

Why it hurts: Wasted effort. No communication.

How to avoid it: Ensure animations are appropriately visible.

26. Visual Pollution
What it is: Unnecessary visual complexity.

Why it hurts: Distraction and cognitive load.

How to avoid it: Keep animations clean and simple.

27. Resource Waste
What it is: Animations that consume unnecessary resources.

Why it hurts: Performance degradation.

How to avoid it: Optimize relentlessly.

28. Inconsistent Language
What it is: Different animations communicating different things.

Why it hurts: Confusion and inconsistency.

How to avoid it: Consistent animation language is essential.

29. Broken Narrative
What it is: Animations that don't tell a coherent story.

Why it hurts: Undermines narrative and engagement.

How to avoid it: Every animation should tell a story.

30. Undefined Failure
What it is: No plan for when animations fail.

Why it hurts: Broken experiences.

How to avoid it: Always define failure states.

31. Forgotten End State
What it is: No clear end state.

Why it hurts: Ambiguity and incompleteness.

How to avoid it: Always define end state.

32. Inconsistent Stagger
What it is: Unpredictable staggering.

Why it hurts: Rhythm disruption.

How to avoid it: Use consistent stagger patterns.

33. Overlapping Effects
What it is: Too many effects in one animation.

Why it hurts: Visual clutter.

How to avoid it: Keep effects minimal.

34. Sensory Overload
What it is: Too much motion and visual stimulation.

Why it hurts: Overwhelms users.

How to avoid it: Maintain animation density limits.

35. Battery Drain
What it is: Animations that drain battery.

Why it hurts: Poor mobile experience.

How to avoid it: Optimize for battery efficiency.

36. Unresponsive Motion
What it is: Motion that doesn't respond to interaction.

Why it hurts: Feels disconnected and broken.

How to avoid it: Motion should respond to interaction.

37. Mechanical Motion
What it is: Motion without organic quality.

Why it hurts: Feels unnatural and cold.

How to avoid it: Use appropriate easing.

38. Overly Organic Motion
What it is: Motion that's too smooth or bouncy.

Why it hurts: Feels unrealistic and distracting.

How to avoid it: Balance natural and mechanical.

39. Unsynchronized Audio
What it is: Motion and audio that don't align.

Why it hurts: Creates dissonance.

How to avoid it: Synchronize audio and motion.

40. Inconsistent Brand Expression
What it is: Motion that doesn't express brand values.

Why it hurts: Weakens brand identity.

How to avoid it: Motion should express brand values.

41. Ignored Context
What it is: Motion that ignores content context.

Why it hurts: Feels irrelevant and disconnected.

How to avoid it: Consider content in animation selection.

42. Unclear Purpose
What it is: Motion with no clear purpose.

Why it hurts: Wasted opportunity.

How to avoid it: Every animation needs a purpose.

43. Unconsidered Emotion
What it is: Motion that ignores emotional impact.

Why it hurts: Missed emotional connection.

How to avoid it: Consider emotional goals.

44. Unclear Narrative
What it is: Motion that doesn't tell a story.

Why it hurts: Missed narrative opportunity.

How to avoid it: Every animation tells a story.

45. Unconsidered Accessibility
What it is: Motion that ignores accessibility.

Why it hurts: Exclusion.

How to avoid it: Accessibility first.

46. Unconsidered Performance
What it is: Motion that ignores performance.

Why it hurts: Poor experience.

How to avoid it: Performance first.

47. Unconsidered Maintainability
What it is: Motion that's hard to maintain.

Why it hurts: Technical debt.

How to avoid it: Keep animations maintainable.

48. Unconsidered Scalability
What it is: Motion that doesn't scale.

Why it hurts: Technical debt and inconsistency.

How to avoid it: Design for scalability.

49. Unconsidered Reviewability
What it is: Motion that's hard to review.

Why it hurts: Quality issues.

How to avoid it: Make animations reviewable.

50. Unconsidered Extensibility
What it is: Motion that's hard to extend.

Why it hurts: Technical debt.

How to avoid it: Design for extension.

51. Unconsidered AI Usability
What it is: Motion that's hard for AI to generate.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent AI output.

How to avoid it: Design for AI understanding.

52. Unconsidered Documentation
What it is: Motion without documentation.

Why it hurts: Knowledge loss.

How to avoid it: Document everything.

53. Unconsidered Training
What it is: Motion without training materials.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent implementation.

How to avoid it: Create training materials.

54. Unconsidered Onboarding
What it is: Motion without onboarding.

Why it hurts: Poor new employee experience.

How to avoid it: Create onboarding materials.

55. Unconsidered Evolution
What it is: Motion without evolution planning.

Why it hurts: Obsolescence.

How to avoid it: Plan for evolution.

56. Unconsidered Testing
What it is: Motion without testing.

Why it hurts: Quality issues.

How to avoid it: Test animations.

57. Unconsidered Monitoring
What it is: Motion without monitoring.

Why it hurts: Performance issues.

How to avoid it: Monitor animations.

58. Unconsidered Analytics
What it is: Motion without analytics.

Why it hurts: Missed insights.

How to avoid it: Track animation effectiveness.

59. Unconsidered User Feedback
What it is: Motion without user feedback.

Why it hurts: Missed improvements.

How to avoid it: Collect user feedback.

60. Unconsidered Design Drift
What it is: Motion that drifts from design system.

Why it hurts: Inconsistency.

How to avoid it: Maintain design system.

61. Unconsidered Code Drift
What it is: Motion that drifts from codebase.

Why it hurts: Technical debt.

How to avoid it: Maintain codebase.

62. Unconsidered Documentation Drift
What it is: Motion that drifts from documentation.

Why it hurts: Knowledge loss.

How to avoid it: Keep documentation current.

63. Unconsidered Recipe Adherence
What it is: Motion that doesn't follow recipes.

Why it hurts: Inconsistency and quality issues.

How to avoid it: Follow recipes exactly.

64. Unconsidered Recipe Evolution
What it is: Recipes that don't evolve.

Why it hurts: Obsolescence.

How to avoid it: Evolve recipes.

65. Unconsidered Recipe Deprecation
What it is: Recipes that aren't deprecated properly.

Why it hurts: Continued use of outdated recipes.

How to avoid it: Deprecate properly.

66. Unconsidered Recipe Replacement
What it is: Recipes replaced without migration.

Why it hurts: Broken experiences.

How to avoid it: Provide migration paths.

67. Unconsidered Recipe Conflicts
What it is: Recipes that conflict.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent experiences.

How to avoid it: Identify and resolve conflicts.

68. Unconsidered Recipe Gaps
What it is: Missing recipes for common needs.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent experiences.

How to avoid it: Identify and fill gaps.

69. Unconsidered Recipe Redundancy
What it is: Redundant recipes.

Why it hurts: Unnecessary complexity.

How to avoid it: Merge redundant recipes.

70. Unconsidered Recipe Incompleteness
What it is: Incomplete recipes.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent application.

How to avoid it: Ensure recipes are complete.

71. Unconsidered Recipe Ambiguity
What it is: Ambiguous recipes.

Why it hurts: Inconsistent application.

How to avoid it: Make recipes unambiguous.

72. Unconsidered Recipe Inaccessibility
What it is: Inaccessible recipes.

Why it hurts: Exclusion.

How to avoid it: Ensure recipes are accessible.

73. Unconsidered Recipe Performance
What it is: Recipes with performance issues.

Why it hurts: Poor experience.

How to avoid it: Ensure recipes are performant.

74. Unconsidered Recipe Maintainability
What it is: Recipes that are hard to maintain.

Why it hurts: Technical debt.

How to avoid it: Ensure recipes are maintainable.

75. Unconsidered Recipe Scalability
What it is: Recipes that don't scale.

Why it hurts: Technical debt.

How to avoid it: Ensure recipes scale.

76. Unconsidered Recipe Reviewability
What it is: Recipes that are hard to review.

Why it hurts: Quality issues.

How to avoid it: Ensure recipes are reviewable.

77. Unconsidered Recipe Extensibility
What it is: Recipes that are hard to extend.
