Examples: Use bot detection that is invisible and relies on heuristics, time-based tokens, or federated identity. If a CAPTCHA is mandated, offer a multi-modal challenge and a reliable fallback like email verification.
Contributor Guidance: Architect authentication to not need CAPTCHAs. If unavoidable, provide equal access.

Principle 56: Third-Party Embeds and Widgets Must Meet Standards
Meaning: Any embedded content from external sources (videos, social media feeds, payment widgets) must be vetted for accessibility and replaced or wrapped if they fall short.
Reason: Inaccessible embeds introduce broken experiences that damage the overall trust. A single inaccessible payment form can prevent a transaction.
Examples: A Stripe payment element is checked for keyboard and screen reader support; if it has issues, we may use a redirect or a custom form. An embedded YouTube video uses YouTube's accessible player.
Contributor Guidance: Maintain an allowlist of accessible third-party components. Test them periodically.

Principle 57: The Accessibility Tree Is Never Broken
Meaning: Developers must understand the impact of CSS display: none, visibility: hidden, aria-hidden, and other techniques that remove content from the accessibility tree, and use them correctly.
Reason: Accidentally hiding essential content from assistive technology while keeping it visible creates a split experience.
Examples: A modal backdrop uses aria-hidden="true" on the main content, but the modal itself remains in the tree. A decorative icon has aria-hidden="true" and no accessible name. Content that is visually hidden but meant for screen readers is correctly shown.
Contributor Guidance: Review every use of aria-hidden and display: none for correctness. Test with accessibility tree inspectors.

Principle 58: All Charts and Data Visualizations Are Perceivable
Meaning: Data visualizations must be accompanied by accessible data tables or textual descriptions that convey the same insights.
Reason: A chart is a visual encoding; blind users need the underlying data or a high-level summary to understand the trends, outliers, and relationships.
Examples: A line chart of server load provides a “View data table” button that reveals a structured table. The chart’s aria-label describes the key trend: “Server load increased by 20% over the last hour.”
Contributor Guidance: Never ship a visualization without a data table equivalent. Generate alt text summaries programmatically where possible.

Principle 59: The Loading Experience Is Accessible
Meaning: Loading indicators, skeleton screens, and progress bars must be announced to screen readers, provide meaningful feedback, and not trap focus.
Reason: A silent, motionless loading state can be interpreted as a broken page. Users need to know the system is working.
Examples: A progress bar uses role="progressbar" and updates aria-valuenow. Skeleton screens are marked as decorative and a live region announces “Loading dashboard…”.
Contributor Guidance: Design loading states to be informative and calm. Ensure that if loading takes too long, a timeout message appears.

Principle 60: Accessibility Is Part of Definition of Done
Meaning: No feature, user story, or bug fix is considered complete until all accessibility acceptance criteria are met and verified.
Reason: Treating accessibility as a separate phase leads to perpetual debt. Integrating it into the definition of done makes it an engineering routine.
Examples: A task’s completion requires passing automated accessibility tests, a keyboard navigation check, and a screen reader check. The code review checklist includes an accessibility section.
Contributor Guidance: Do not allow workarounds or “will fix later.” Technical debt for accessibility is treated as a P0 bug.

Keyboard Navigation Philosophy
Keyboard navigation is not a fallback for mouse users; it is a primary interaction mode. At Zenuxs, we design for the keyboard first, then enhance for pointer and touch. This order reflects the reality that keyboard interactions map cleanly to other assistive technologies: a screen reader user navigates primarily via keyboard commands; a switch device user generates keystrokes; voice control software often emulates keyboard actions. A keyboard-first interface is inherently more accessible and more robust.

Focus order must be logical and predictable. It follows the reading order of the content, typically left to right, top to bottom. When visual layout diverges from source order, we either restructure the DOM or manage tabindex to maintain a natural sequence. We never use positive tabindex values that create a custom order disconnected from the visual flow. The focus order should feel like a well-organized narrative, guiding the user through the interface without surprises.

Focus visibility is a non-negotiable quality. Every interactive element that receives focus must display a distinct outline or highlight that is perceivable by sighted keyboard users. The default browser focus indicator is often insufficient; we design a custom focus ring that is integrated into the design system, with a minimum contrast ratio of 3:1 against all possible backgrounds. This indicator is never removed globally; instances where outline: none is used are accompanied by an enhanced custom focus style. The focus indicator is part of the brand, signaling clarity and deliberate design.

Logical navigation relies on semantic structure and landmarks. Users can jump between regions (banner, navigation, main, complementary) with assistive technology shortcut keys. Within each region, tab stops are limited to interactive elements; static text is not in the tab order, but is accessible via screen reader browse mode. We minimize the number of tab stops to reduce fatigue; groups of related actions can be combined into a single stop with arrow key navigation (e.g., toolbars, menus, tabs) following the established patterns.

Escape behavior is a fundamental safety mechanism. In every context, pressing Escape should safely dismiss the current overlay, close the current menu, or cancel the current operation, returning the user to a stable state. This behavior must be consistent across all components. Escape never performs a destructive action; it is a “get me out of here” command. Likewise, the Enter key activates the primary action; Space toggles state; Tab moves forward; Shift+Tab moves backward. These conventions are sacred and never overridden without a critical, documented reason.

Shortcut philosophy balances power and safety. Application-wide shortcuts (e.g., open command palette, navigate to terminal) use modifier keys (Ctrl/Cmd) to avoid conflicts with text input. Single-key shortcuts are avoided in editable contexts. All shortcuts are discoverable via the command palette and a dedicated keyboard shortcut help dialog. Users can customize shortcuts to suit their needs and avoid conflicts with assistive technology modifiers. We never ship shortcuts that are hidden or unchangeable.

Tab navigation should be smooth and uninterrupted. We avoid focus traps except in modal contexts where intentional. When a modal opens, focus moves into it, and Tab cycles only within the modal until it is dismissed. When a modal closes, focus returns to the element that triggered it. Dropdowns and autocomplete lists that expand on interaction are managed similarly, allowing arrow key navigation within the list and Escape to close, with focus returning to the input.

Arrow navigation extends to composite widgets. Grids, tree views, list boxes, and tab lists all support arrow key navigation within the group, often following two-dimensional patterns (up/down, left/right). The system must handle RTL (right-to-left) languages correctly, flipping the direction of horizontal arrow keys as appropriate. Home and End keys jump to the first and last items; Page Up/Down scrolls or moves by pages.

Dialog navigation is explicitly defined. When a dialog opens, focus is set to the first focusable element, or to the dialog container if it contains only text. A descriptive heading labels the dialog, and the dialog’s content is accessible via browse mode. The dialog traps focus; pressing Tab at the last focusable element moves focus to the first, and Shift+Tab at the first moves to the last. The dialog must be dismissable by Escape and by an explicit close button, both of which are reachable via keyboard.

Command Palette navigation is a keystroke-first experience. Opened by a global shortcut, the palette provides a search-driven interface to all commands, actions, and navigation targets. Keyboard interaction includes fuzzy search, arrow key selection, Enter to execute, Escape to dismiss, and clear announcement of results to screen readers. The palette itself is fully accessible: input is properly labeled, live region updates announce the number of results, and executed commands confirm their outcome.

Terminal navigation extends beyond the basics. The Zenuxs terminal supports standard shell keyboard conventions (Ctrl+C to interrupt, Ctrl+D to exit, Tab for completion) while ensuring that screen readers can announce prompt text, command output, and completion suggestions. The terminal is not a black box; it participates in the same accessibility infrastructure as the rest of the UI. Focus management within the terminal is predictable; the terminal is a single tab stop, and internal cursor movement is handled by the terminal emulator, not by the browser's focus system. We ensure that screen readers can track the cursor position and read output incrementally.

Screen Reader Philosophy
A screen reader user does not "view" a page; they listen to a linear stream of information synthesized from the accessibility tree. This tree is built from our semantic markup, ARIA attributes, and the computed properties of each element. The quality of a screen reader experience depends entirely on the accuracy, clarity, and conciseness of that tree. Zenuxs treats the accessibility tree as a first-class API surface, deserving the same rigor as our visual layout.

Semantic meaning is the foundation. We use native HTML elements wherever possible because they come with implicit roles, states, and keyboard interactions recognized by all screen readers. A <nav> is a navigation landmark; a <button> is a button; a <table> is a table. When custom components are necessary, we meticulously apply ARIA roles, states, and properties, and we test the resulting experience across multiple screen reader and browser combinations. We never misuse ARIA to compensate for poor semantics; ARIA is a polyfill for things HTML cannot express.

Landmarks provide the high-level map of a page. Every Zenuxs page is structured with landmarks: banner for the site header, navigation for primary and secondary nav, main for the unique content, complementary for sidebars, contentinfo for the footer, and region for other significant areas. Each landmark is uniquely labeled if there are multiple of the same type (e.g., “Main navigation,” “Contextual sidebar”). This allows a screen reader user to summon a list of landmarks and jump directly to the section they need, bypassing repetitive chrome. Landmarks turn a flat page into a navigable document.

Reading order must match the intended sequence of content consumption. We avoid CSS techniques that reorder elements visually but leave the DOM in a nonsensical order. When a layout places a sidebar before the main content in the source but after visually, it creates confusion. We either adjust the source order or use layout methods that preserve a logical reading flow. The reading order is tested by disabling CSS and listening to the screen reader’s read-all command; it should tell a coherent story.

Announcements bridge the gap between dynamic UI changes and the blind user’s awareness. When state changes occur asynchronously — a message arrives, a count updates, a background process finishes — we use live regions (aria-live) to inform the user without forcing focus movement. The role="status" announces politely, queued after the current utterance. role="alert" interrupts immediately for critical messages. We carefully tune verbosity: announcements should be meaningful, not noisy. An autocomplete that announces every keystroke’s filtered results would be overwhelming; instead, it announces the number of suggestions after a short debounce.

Labels provide the identity of controls. Every interactive element must have a computed accessible name. For form fields, we prefer explicit <label> elements. For icon buttons, we use aria-label or hidden text. For images that convey information, the alt attribute serves as the label. For complex widgets, aria-labelledby can reference multiple elements. We ensure that visible text is included in the accessible name to support voice control. Labels are concise, descriptive, and consistent across the product.

Descriptions provide additional context without cluttering the label. Using aria-describedby, we can associate help text, error messages, and formatting hints with inputs. A screen reader will announce the label, then the description, giving the user a richer understanding. For example, a password field might have a description: “Must be at least 12 characters and include a symbol.” This information is available on demand, not forced on every tab stop.

Dynamic updates must be managed with care. Single-page application frameworks can cause screen readers to miss route changes because there is no full page reload. We implement a client-side router that, upon navigation, announces the new page title (via document.title update and a live region) and moves focus to the beginning of the main content. Similarly, when a user activates a “show more” button, we move focus to the newly revealed content or announce its availability. We never leave the user in silence wondering if something happened.

Navigation landmarks should be complemented with skip links. A “Skip to main content” link, visible on focus, is the first interactive element on every page. This allows keyboard and screen reader users to bypass navigation headers directly. In addition to global skip links, we may implement intra-page skip links for long content areas, but only where they genuinely improve efficiency.

Context preservation means that when a user moves between views or activates a control, they do not lose their place unnecessarily. For example, after deleting an item from a list, focus moves to the next item or back to the list container, not to the top of the page. When returning from a detail view to a list, the scroll position and the previously focused item are restored. This spatial memory is critical for power users and screen reader users alike, who build a mental map of the interface.

How screen readers experience Zenuxs can be described as a well-structured, responsive conversation. The user issues commands (by keystroke or gesture), and the screen reader responds with relevant information: the role, name, state, and value of the focused element, plus contextual hints. Because we have invested heavily in semantic correctness, minimal dynamic noise, and predictable focus management, the screen reader user feels in control. They can operate the terminal, query the AI, browse documentation, and manage projects with the same depth as any other user. The experience is not a simulation of a visual interface; it is a first-class mode of interaction designed for the ears and hands, not a translation of a visual afterthought.

Motion Accessibility
Motion in Zenuxs serves to create a sense of place, to guide attention, and to provide delight. But motion can also cause physical harm. Vestibular disorders, migraine triggers, and sensory sensitivities affect a significant portion of the population. We treat motion as a potentially hazardous property and give users absolute control over their experience.

Reduced motion is not a “lesser” experience; it is an equally valid design. When a user enables the prefers-reduced-motion: reduce setting at the OS level, Zenuxs respects it completely. All non-essential animations are disabled. Page transitions happen instantly. Autoplay carousels stop cycling. Decorative parallax effects are removed. Motion that communicates state change (e.g., a menu opening) is replaced with a subtle opacity or instantaneous transition, preserving the information without the physical movement. We never serve a broken or degraded layout when motion is reduced.

Alternative interactions replace any feature whose primary affordance relies on motion. For instance, if a tutorial uses animated gestures to demonstrate a drag-and-drop action, we provide a static illustration with text steps and a keyboard-accessible simulation. If a loading indicator is a spinning animation, we also display a text percentage or a static progress bar. The principle: motion may enhance meaning, but it must not be the sole carrier of meaning.

Animation substitution is engineered into the design system. Our motion tokens define not just durations and easing curves, but also a reduced-motion counterpart. For example, a slide-in transition token maps to transform: none; opacity: 1; when reduced motion is active. This substitution is handled at the theme level, so individual component developers need only reference the token. This ensures consistency and prevents accidental animation leakage.

Safe transitions are those that do not involve large-scale movement, parallax, or 3D spatial effects. We avoid bouncing, spinning, and zooming animations in critical UI. When subtle micro-interactions are used (e.g., a button press effect), they are carefully bounded, short in duration (under 200ms), and do not cause layout shift. We test these with the reduced-motion flag and ensure the component still conveys the feedback.

Motion sensitivity goes beyond vestibular issues. Some users with ADHD or autism may find constant animation distracting or overwhelming. We provide an in-app preference to disable all animations even if the OS does not have the setting. This toggle covers custom animations, GIF autoplay, and animated emojis. The default state for new users respects the OS preference, but the user can override it.

Vestibular considerations extend to spatial consistency. Animation that violates expectations — like moving the user’s viewpoint without a clear cause — can be disorienting. We avoid animated scroll-jacking, unexpected layout shifts (CLS is also a performance metric), and parallax that creates a false sense of depth. Our UI remains grounded; elements generally stay where they are unless the user initiates a movement (scrolling, navigating).

Timing flexibility: even when motion is enabled, users can adjust the speed. A “motion speed” slider allows scaling animation durations from 0.5x to 2x. This accommodates users who want motion but slower (for better tracking) or faster (for efficiency). All animations are defined with durations in relative terms that can be multiplied by a global factor.

User preferences for motion are part of the accessibility profile. We store them, sync them, and apply them before the first frame renders, eliminating the flash of unwanted animation. We regularly review new animations and assess their potential impact, using tools like the vestibular motion checker.

Visual Accessibility
Visual accessibility ensures that the information and controls presented on screen are perceivable by people with low vision, color blindness, or situational impairments like bright sunlight or aging eyes. A visually inaccessible interface can be completely unusable even if the underlying semantics are sound, because sighted keyboard users rely on visual cues to navigate.

Contrast is the fundamental requirement. All text, icons, and focus indicators must meet minimum contrast ratios against their backgrounds. Our design system defines foreground and background color pairs that are mathematically verified. We do not rely on the designer’s eye; we use automated contrast checking in both design tools and CI pipelines. For large text (24px or larger, or 19px bold), the threshold is at least 3:1; for normal text, at least 4.5:1. UI components and graphical objects that convey information must meet 3:1. We maintain an elevated contrast palette for users who need even higher contrast, going beyond 7:1 for body text. This palette is selectable as a theme.

Typography is engineered for legibility. We choose typefaces with clear letterforms, distinct ascenders and descenders, and generous x-height. Font sizes follow a modular scale that ensures readability at default and enlarged settings. Line height is set to at least 1.5 for body text; letter spacing is adjustable via user preference but defaults to a balanced, readable measure. We avoid full justification, which can create “rivers of white” that disrupt reading for dyslexic users. Text never overlaps or truncates when the user overrides font family or size.

Readable hierarchy uses more than font size. Headings, lists, blockquotes, and other structural elements have clear visual differentiation through spacing, weight, and subtle color changes, but never rely on color alone to convey hierarchy. An <h2> is distinct from an <h3> even in grayscale. Our design tokens for typography are mapped to semantic roles, ensuring that any re-theming maintains the hierarchy.

Color independence means that no information is lost in monochrome. Graphs, status indicators, and maps use patterns, shapes, labels, or secondary encodings. For example, a traffic light status indicator might include the text “OK”, “Warning”, “Critical” alongside the colored circle. Links are underlined by default, not just colored blue. This benefits not only color-blind users but also anyone using a black-and-white e-reader or a sunlight-washed screen.

Visual feedback for interactions must be multi-sensory where possible. A button press shows a change in appearance (scale, background, border) but also, if appropriate, triggers a screen reader announcement. However, visual feedback alone must be sufficient for sighted keyboard users who cannot hear. The focus indicator is part of visual feedback; we design it to be unmistakable. Hover states are also distinct but never the only indicator; focus and hover can differ.

Focus indicators deserve deep attention. The system provides a global focus style that is a highly visible outline or box-shadow with a color that contrasts against any background. In complex components, we may augment this with background changes or scaling. The focus indicator always has a minimum area and thickness. We never suppress focus outlines except when providing an even more obvious custom indicator, and we never rely on color alone for that indicator. It is tested against all theme backgrounds.

Scalable interfaces require layout that responds to zoom. At 200% browser zoom, content reflows to a single column without horizontal scrolling. Text remains readable; images scale appropriately; interactive elements remain usable. We avoid fixed-width containers that overflow. We use responsive images and SVG that scale cleanly. The terminal emulator supports font scaling and window resizing without breaking the grid.

Zoom support also means that when users rely on screen magnification software (like ZoomText), the interface does not break. The magnified portion should still contain all the contextual information; tooltips and popups must not be cut off or appear off-screen. We design with a “focus area” principle: any action that opens a flyout should ensure that flyout is positioned within the viewport and can be scrolled into view. CSS scroll-margin and anchor positioning techniques are used to guarantee visibility.

Cognitive Accessibility
Cognitive accessibility addresses the mental effort required to understand, navigate, and operate Zenuxs. Users with learning disabilities, attention disorders, memory impairments, or those under stress or fatigue benefit from an interface that minimizes cognitive load and supports clear mental models.

Reducing cognitive load begins with simplicity. We strive to present only the necessary information at the right time. Cluttered interfaces overwhelm; we use progressive disclosure to reveal advanced options only when they are relevant. The default view of any feature shows the most common path; power-user features are tucked into menus or toggles, accessible but not intrusive. The terminal follows the same philosophy: common commands are short and discoverable; complex options are well-documented but not forced.

Predictable navigation creates a consistent spatial model. Primary navigation stays in the same location across pages. The command palette provides a universal entry point to any action, reducing the need to memorize visual layouts. Breadcrumbs show location hierarchy. Back buttons behave consistently. The AI chat panel and terminal are always in reach through persistent controls. When the user activates a link, the outcome matches the label; there are no “mystery meat” interactions.

Clear terminology uses plain, unambiguous language. We avoid jargon where possible, and when technical terms are necessary, we provide tooltips or progressive explanations. Button labels use action-oriented verbs (“Save”, “Cancel”, “Send”) that precisely describe the outcome. Confirmation dialogs spell out consequences in plain language: “Delete this project? This cannot be undone.” AI-generated responses default to clear, helpful language, with the option to expand technical detail.

Progressive disclosure is a core pattern. Advanced search filters are collapsed behind a “Filters” button. AI tool execution details are hidden behind an expandable section. CLI tools offer a verbose flag for extra output. This allows new users to succeed with minimal information and experts to dig deeper without cluttering the base experience. The screen reader experience similarly benefits: the initial announcement might be a summary, with the ability to drill into details.

Simple workflows are designed to minimize steps. We analyze critical task flows (creating a project, deploying a site, querying AI) and remove unnecessary friction. Defaults are safe and intelligent. Wizards guide complex sequences but never trap users. An activity can be paused and resumed later. The system remembers incomplete work and offers to restore it, reducing cognitive burden.

Error prevention is better than error messages. We anticipate common mistakes and design to prevent them. Form fields use input constraints and on-the-fly validation. Dangerous actions are harder to reach (but not impossible). Confirmation dialogs have the “safe” option as the default focused element (e.g., “Cancel” is focused, not “Delete”). The terminal can warn about destructive commands (like rm -rf), requiring explicit confirmation.

Recognition over recall is a fundamental UX heuristic. Users should not have to remember information from one part of the interface to use another. Search suggestions remind users of available options. Recently used commands appear in the terminal history. The command palette displays all possible actions with fuzzy matching. The AI chat provides context-aware suggestions. Documentation is interlinked so related concepts are one click away, not something to remember and search for.

Mental models must be supported by the interface structure. The file tree in the IDE, the project hierarchy, the AI conversation threading — all map to real-world or well-known digital metaphors. Consistency in behavior reinforces these models. If a drag-and-drop operation works in the file tree, it should work in the task board too. If clicking a folder expands it, arrow keys should do the same. The system behaves the way the user expects, based on past experience with Zenuxs and with other quality software.

Interaction Accessibility
Every interaction point — buttons, forms, inputs, search, navigation, command palette, terminal, AI chat, dialogs, notifications, tooltips — must be designed and built to a unified accessibility standard. The following expectations apply universally.

Buttons must have clear, actionable labels. They must be reachable and activatable by keyboard (Enter/Space). They must convey their state (disabled, pressed, loading) through both visual design and accessibility properties. Toggle buttons must have aria-pressed. Buttons that trigger an immediate action (like copy to clipboard) must provide feedback (tooltip or announcement). No <div> with an onClick handler that lacks role, tabindex, and keyboard handling.

Forms are the gateway to user input. Every input must have a persistent visible label, programmatically associated. Required fields are indicated with both a red asterisk and the word “required” in the label or description, not just color. Validation errors appear inline, with clear text linking the error to the field, and are announced. Grouped inputs (like radio buttons, checkboxes) use <fieldset> and <legend>. Forms must be submittable by keyboard (Enter) and the submit action must be clearly identifiable. Multi-step forms announce step changes.

Inputs of type text, password, email, etc., support autocomplete attributes. Custom inputs (date pickers, sliders, color pickers) follow established ARIA patterns. The terminal input is part of this: it is a text input that supports screen reader announcement of the prompt, and its output is a live region.

Search is a critical interaction. The search input has a clear label, a submit button, and the search is not triggered automatically after every keystroke (unless designed as a live filter, in which case the number of results is announced). Search results are structured as a list with clear headings and a status message like “12 results for 'accessibility'.” Each result is a link or button, and the list can be navigated with arrow keys.

Navigation elements (menus, tabs, accordions) follow their ARIA patterns precisely. Menus are accessible: the menu button discloses a list of menu items, arrow keys navigate within, Escape closes, and focus returns to the button. Tabs use the tabs pattern with proper roles. Accordions use disclosure pattern with aria-expanded. Navigation must never rely on hover alone to reveal submenus; focus or click must also trigger them.

The Command Palette is a model of keyboard-driven interaction. It is opened by a global shortcut, focus moves to a search input, results appear in a list, selection is by arrow keys, execution by Enter. Every state change — search start, result count, no results, action performed — is announced via live region. The palette’s input has a label, and the list is marked as a listbox with options.

The Terminal is interactive text. It must be screen-reader friendly. We achieve this by ensuring the terminal is a single focusable element with a clear role, and using virtual buffers or accessible output logs that can be browsed. Commands and output are announced as they appear. The terminal supports standard readline keybindings, and we provide an accessible mode that toggles between line-by-line reading and browsable output with screen reader review commands. Color schemes are customizable and must meet contrast.

AI Chat is a rich interactive component. Messages are a live region (aria-live="polite"), with each new message appended and announced. The user input is a text field with a send button. The AI’s streaming response is managed so that the screen reader does not re-announce the entire message on every token; we use a debounced live region or chunk announcements. Tool calls and results are nested in expandable sections, announced as they execute. The chat history is navigable with arrow keys, and each message is a discrete article with a labeled author (“You said: …”, “Zenuxs AI said: …”).

Dialogs we’ve covered: focus trap, labeled, dismissable. Notifications (toasts) use role="status" or alert appropriately. They are temporary but should be user-dismissable. They never steal focus. They appear in a consistent location and do not block interaction with the underlying page unless urgent.

Tooltips appear on hover and focus. They must not contain interactive content unless they are designed as persistent popovers with hover/focus support. Pure tooltips are for short descriptive text; they should not be required for understanding the interface, only for supplementary info. They are implemented with the tooltip role or aria-describedby and are announced by screen readers after a short delay. Tooltips never contain critical instructions.

Every interaction is expected to be accessible by default. The design system encapsulates these behaviors, so a developer placing a <Button variant="primary"> gets accessibility for free. When custom interactions are necessary, they are built once, tested thoroughly, and added to the system.

AI Accessibility
Zenuxs integrates AI deeply — as a conversational partner, a code assistant, a terminal copilot, and an autonomous agent. AI interactions present unique accessibility challenges because the system must communicate reasoning, streaming text, tool execution, and planning in a way that is perceivable, understandable, and controllable for all users.

Accessible AI conversations begin with clear authorship and structure. Every message in a chat is marked up as an article with a heading indicating the speaker (“You” or “Zenuxs”). The chat history is navigable by message, and the user can jump to the latest message with a keyboard shortcut. Screen reader users can browse the conversation linearly, hearing who said what.

Readable reasoning: when the AI performs chain-of-thought reasoning or planning, that process must be exposed optionally. Some users want the full reasoning; others find it overwhelming. We provide a “Show reasoning” toggle that, when expanded, reveals the AI’s internal monologue in a structured, accessible disclosure. When collapsed, a summary is provided. The reasoning content is formatted with clear headings and code blocks, all fully accessible.

Understandable streaming: as the AI generates a response token by token, the screen reader experience must not be chaotic. We buffer incoming tokens and update a live region with complete sentences or clauses, rather than every character. The aria-live region is set to polite, and the updates are throttled to avoid flooding the speech queue. A visual cursor or pulsing indicator shows that generation is in progress. The user can interrupt the generation with a “Stop” button (reachable by keyboard) or by pressing Escape.

Accessible tool execution: when the AI decides to run a tool (browse the web, execute a command, read a file), that action must be announced. A live region says “Zenuxs is searching the documentation…” or “Running terminal command.” The tool’s input and output are displayed in an expandable region with appropriate ARIA roles. If a tool fails, the error is announced and explained. The user can cancel a running tool.

Accessible planning: when the AI generates a multi-step plan, it is presented as a task list with checkable items, each announced as a step. The current active step is highlighted and announced as it begins. This structure helps users with cognitive disabilities follow along and understand progress. The plan can be navigated with arrow keys.

Accessible memory: the AI’s persistent memory (knowledge about the user, preferences, past conversations) is accessible through a dedicated interface. Users can review, edit, or delete memories. This interface is fully keyboard and screen reader accessible. AI responses that reference memory can cite the specific memory item, and the citation is an expandable section.

Accessible documentation: AI can generate documentation, code explanations, and tutorials. All generated content must meet the same accessibility standards as human-authored content: proper heading structure, alt text for images (if any), readable code blocks with syntax highlighting that has sufficient contrast, and an accessible structure. The AI is instructed to produce accessible content.

How AI should communicate with all users: the default tone is inclusive, respectful, and clear. The AI avoids ableist language or assumptions about the user’s abilities. When providing instructions that involve visual tasks (e.g., “click the red button”), it also describes the button by its label or position. The AI is capable of adapting its output length and complexity based on user request, but always maintains clarity.

The AI interaction model is an extension of the product’s overall accessibility commitment. It is not a separate, less-accessible magic; it is built on the same semantic foundations, keyboard principles, and screen reader considerations. The AI is a fellow user of the interface, and its output is just another form of content that must be made universally perceivable.

CLI Accessibility
The command-line interface is a core part of Zenuxs, used by developers, operators, and power users. CLI accessibility is often an afterthought in the industry; we make it a priority because blind developers, developers with motor disabilities, and those who prefer text-based interaction deserve first-class tooling.

Readable output: CLI tools must output text that is scannable both visually and by screen readers. We use clear, consistent formatting: headings, indentation, and separators. Long output is paginated if interactive, or provides a flag like --no-pager for assistive tech. Tables are drawn with ASCII art but also offered in machine-readable formats (--json, --csv) that can be piped into tools that generate accessible representations. We avoid unnecessary animation in the terminal (like spinner glyphs that constantly change), offering a static progress indicator instead.

Color independence: CLI output often uses colors to convey status (green for success, red for error). We always provide an alternative: the word “ERROR:” or “SUCCESS:” precedes the message, and we support a --no-color flag. The Zenuxs terminal allows users to customize or disable colors. The default color palette meets contrast standards, and high-contrast themes are available.

Screen reader compatibility: terminal emulators present text in a way that screen readers can access. In web-based terminals, we ensure the underlying DOM is a live region that announces new output. We avoid using canvas for terminal rendering; if we use a custom terminal renderer, it must expose the text to the accessibility tree. For native terminals, we output to standard streams and ensure that common screen readers (like VoiceOver’s text area interaction, or Linux’s Speakup) can read the terminal buffer.

Keyboard workflow: CLI is keyboard-native by definition, but we ensure that shortcuts do not conflict with screen reader commands. If the terminal emulator is web-based, we intercept keystrokes only when the terminal is focused, and we provide an escape hatch to release focus (Escape or a specific key). Custom CLI interactive prompts (like inquirer) must be fully keyboard-navigable and announce changes.

Command discoverability: every CLI tool has a built-in --help that outputs clear, structured documentation. The help output uses headings for subcommands and options, making it easy to navigate with screen reader heading commands. We also provide a zxn help command that opens the full documentation in a searchable, accessible format. Tab completion provides audible feedback (via terminal bell or announcement) of available options.

Output hierarchy: complex output (like zxn project list) is formatted as a table with column headers. For screen readers, we pipe this output through a tool that reads column headers as you navigate cells. We encourage the use of structured data formats for programmatic consumption, but the default human-readable output must still be structured enough for a screen reader user to comprehend.

Terminal users deserve excellent accessibility because the terminal is a power tool. By making it accessible, we ensure that developers with disabilities can automate, script, and interact with Zenuxs at the deepest level without barriers.

Documentation Accessibility
Documentation is a critical part of the product; if a user cannot read the docs, they cannot use the software. All Zenuxs documentation, whether on the docs website, inside the terminal help, or generated by AI, follows the same accessibility standards as the application.

Readable documentation: text is written in plain, clear language. We follow a style guide that prioritizes short sentences, active voice, and unambiguous terminology. Code examples are surrounded by explanatory prose that describes what the code does and why, so that readers who cannot parse the code visually can still understand. Diagrams have alt text and, where possible, a long description.

Navigation: the documentation site uses a consistent layout with a sidebar navigation that is a <nav> landmark, a main content area, and a table of contents on the right (which is complementary). The sidebar is collapsible and its state is preserved. All links have meaningful text, never just “click here.” The table of contents is generated from headings and is itself a list of links, allowing jump navigation.

Search: documentation search is prominent and accessible. It supports keyboard navigation through results, announces the number of hits, and each result includes a snippet with the matched term highlighted (with text emphasis, not just color).

Examples: code examples must be accessible. We use syntax highlighting that meets contrast requirements. The highlighting must not be the sole indicator of code structure; the code itself is text and can be read by screen readers. Interactive examples (like live code editors) must be keyboard-operable and their output must be accessible.

Headings: documentation pages have a clear, logical heading hierarchy. The page title is an <h1>, major sections <h2>, subsections <h3>, and so on. This allows screen reader users to scan the page and jump to sections. We never use heading elements for visual styling.

Code blocks: are wrapped in <pre><code> and are focusable for screen readers that want to read them line by line. For long code blocks, we provide a copy button with accessible feedback. We also offer an option to expand/collapse large code blocks.

Interactive examples: when a page includes an embedded terminal, command palette, or AI chat widget for demonstration, those widgets must themselves be fully accessible. They are iframes with proper titles, and they maintain keyboard isolation so as not to trap focus.

Progressive learning: documentation is structured to support different levels of expertise. Getting started guides are simple and linear. Advanced references are dense but navigable. All paths are accessible; a keyboard or screen reader user should be able to follow any tutorial without friction.

Error Recovery
Errors happen. The measure of an accessible system is not the absence of errors, but how gracefully it handles them. Accessible error recovery builds user confidence because it demonstrates that the system has their back.

Helpful errors: an error message must explain, in plain language, what went wrong, why it happened, and what the user can do about it. “Error 500” is not helpful. “We couldn’t save your project because the server is temporarily unavailable. Please wait a moment and try again, or contact support if the problem persists.” is helpful. Technical details can be included in a collapsible section for advanced users.

Recovery guidance: error messages should include specific, actionable steps. If a network call fails, offer a Retry button. If a form field is invalid, explain the required format. If a command fails due to missing dependencies, tell the user how to install them. Always assume the user wants to fix the problem and needs guidance.

Retry: any action that fails transiently should provide an easy retry mechanism. The retry button must be keyboard accessible and should not lose the user’s previously entered data. Retry can be automatic in some cases, but the user must be informed.

Undo: wherever possible, actions that change state should be undoable. Deletion, reordering, and editing should have an undo function (Ctrl+Z, an Undo button, or a temporary toast with Undo). The undo action itself must be accessible and announced. A robust undo system gives users the confidence to explore.

Confirmation: dangerous actions require confirmation, but confirmation dialogs must be clear, not anxiety-inducing. The dialog should describe the action, list the affected items, and have distinctly labeled buttons (e.g., “Delete project” vs. “Keep project”). The safe or default choice should receive initial focus, but the destructive option should still be reachable.

Warnings: when an action could have unintended consequences but is not immediately destructive (e.g., changing settings that affect many users), we provide a warning that must be acknowledged. Warnings are announced as alerts but do not force focus away from the current task unless necessary.

Validation: client-side validation provides immediate, polite feedback. As a user fills out a field, we validate on blur or after a debounce, and surface errors adjacent to the field. We never clear valid input on error. The error message is linked to the field via aria-describedby and the field can be styled with a visual indicator that is not solely color. Server-side errors after submission must focus the error summary or first errored field.

Safe defaults: the system’s initial state should be the least destructive. Checkboxes that enable risky features should be unchecked by default. Buttons that delete data should require an additional confirmation. The terminal’s --force flag should be explicit and documented. Safe defaults reduce the chance that an accidental keystroke causes harm.

Accessibility improves confidence: when users know that mistakes are recoverable, that they won’t lose their work, and that the system will explain what happened, they are more willing to explore features and use the product to its full potential. This is especially important for users who may take longer to complete tasks or who are using unfamiliar assistive technologies.

Responsive Accessibility
Zenuxs works on desktop, tablet, mobile, and any screen size in between. Responsive design is an accessibility concern because fixed-width, desktop-only layouts are exclusionary to users who only have a mobile device, or who need to magnify the screen, or who use a small laptop.

Desktop: the full-featured experience with multi-column layouts, advanced keyboard shortcuts, and dense information. Accessibility is not sacrificed for complexity; we ensure that even complex dashboards are navigable by keyboard and screen reader through careful use of landmarks and focus management.

Tablet: touch is the primary input, but external keyboards are common. We ensure that touch targets are large enough and that the interface does not rely on hover. The layout adapts gracefully, with collapsible sidebars and a focus on content. The on-screen keyboard must not obscure focused inputs; we manage viewport and scrolling.

Mobile: the interface must be operable with one hand, with touch, and with a screen reader (VoiceOver on iOS, TalkBack on Android). We test gesture accessibility: all swipe actions have alternative button controls. The terminal on mobile is simplified but still usable, with a command input and output area. The AI chat is fully functional. Navigation collapses into a bottom tab bar or hamburger menu, both keyboard and screen reader friendly.

Touch: touch interactions must not trap users. Drag-and-drop must have alternative controls. Long-press actions should not be the only way to access a feature; they should be shortcuts. Touch-specific gestures (pinch, spread) have button equivalents. Touch feedback (like ripples) is optional and respects reduced motion.

Stylus: stylus input is treated as a pointer, but we must ensure that fine motor control is not required. Targets should be large enough for a stylus, and precision should not be assumed.

Keyboard: on mobile, external keyboards (Bluetooth) are supported. The focus order must still be logical, and keyboard shortcuts that make sense on mobile (like Cmd+K for command palette) should still work. The mobile layout must not hide focus indicators.

Large screens: ultra-wide displays should not cause content to stretch beyond readability. We constrain line length to a comfortable measure (60-80 characters) and use max-width containers. Extra space can be used for companion panels (like documentation side-by-side with code), but the user must be able to control the layout. High-resolution screens must not make text too small by default; we use scalable units.

Small screens: the interface must be functional at 320px width. Content reflows, navigation simplifies, but no functionality is lost. The terminal can be used with a minimalist layout. The command palette is a critical navigation tool on small screens because visible navigation is minimal.

Accessibility across devices means a single, adaptive codebase that delivers equivalent functionality and accessibility regardless of form factor. We do not maintain a separate “mobile site” with reduced features; it is all responsive.

Accessibility Performance
Performance is an accessibility requirement. Slow-loading pages, janky interactions, and unstable layouts disproportionately affect users with disabilities, users on low-end devices, and users in regions with limited connectivity. When a page takes too long to load, a screen reader user may hear nothing, assume the page is broken, and leave. When layout shifts unexpectedly, a user with a motor disability may click the wrong button. When scrolling is sluggish, a keyboard user struggles to navigate efficiently.

Fast loading: we optimize for the first meaningful paint and time to interactive. The semantic foundation of the page (the HTML) is server-rendered and arrives quickly, so that assistive technology can begin processing content even while JavaScript bundles are loading. Critical CSS is inlined; non-critical CSS is deferred but does not cause layout shifts. We prioritize loading of interactive elements that are essential for navigation (like skip links and main landmarks).

Low latency: interactive response must be near-instant. Button presses, focus changes, and live region updates should not have a perceivable delay. We batch DOM updates and use efficient rendering strategies to avoid jank. The terminal and AI streaming are optimized for high-frequency updates, but they must not block the main thread, which could prevent screen reader announcements from firing in a timely manner.

Stable layout: cumulative layout shift (CLS) is not only an SEO metric; it is a usability disaster. We reserve space for images, ads, and dynamic content to prevent sudden movement. This is particularly important for users with magnified screens or those using switch devices where the activation target might move between scanning and selection. We define explicit dimensions and use skeleton screens that match the final layout.

Reduced waiting: long operations provide progress indicators and are cancellable. We avoid synchronous blocking operations on the main thread. If an AI response will take seconds, we show an immediate partial response or a progress estimate. The terminal for long-running commands streams output incrementally. Loading states are communicated to assistive technology as described earlier.

Predictable rendering: we avoid layout thrashing and style recalculations that cause visual flicker. The interface should feel solid, not like it’s still loading pieces. For screen reader users, we avoid repaints that might cause the virtual buffer to reload, which can lose the user’s reading position.

Offline resilience: accessibility must not depend on a constant internet connection. Progressive web app capabilities allow the core interface, including documentation and basic terminal, to work offline. AI features that require connectivity gracefully degrade with clear status announcements.

Performance improves accessibility because it reduces the effort required to operate the interface. A fast, responsive system respects the user’s time and reduces cognitive load. When we treat performance as an accessibility feature, we commit to optimizing not just for benchmarks, but for the real human experience.

Accessibility Review Framework
The following questions are used in design reviews, code reviews, and QA to evaluate accessibility. Each must be satisfied before a feature is considered done.

1. Can all tasks be completed using only a keyboard?

Purpose: Verify keyboard operability.

Reason: If a task cannot be done without a mouse, keyboard-only and assistive tech users are blocked.

Evaluation Method: Unplug the mouse. Navigate to the feature and complete the primary workflow using Tab, Enter, Space, arrow keys, Escape.

Acceptance Criteria: 100% of the workflow is achievable without mouse or touch; no action is unreachable or impossible.

2. Is the focus order logical and does it follow visual reading order?

Purpose: Ensure navigation matches user expectations.

Reason: A jumbled focus order disorients users and may cause them to miss controls.

Evaluation Method: Tab through the page from top to bottom and observe the sequence. Compare with visual layout.

Acceptance Criteria: Focus sequence flows naturally left-to-right, top-to-bottom without unexpected jumps. No element is skipped or unreachable.

3. Is focus always visible with a distinct indicator?

Purpose: Confirm sighted keyboard users can track focus.

Reason: Without a visible indicator, navigation is guesswork.

Evaluation Method: Tab through all interactive elements. Check focus indicator visibility against all background colors, including in hover and active states.

Acceptance Criteria: Focus indicator meets 3:1 contrast, is never hidden, and is aesthetically coherent with the design.

4. Does every interactive element have a descriptive, accessible name?

Purpose: Guarantee screen readers announce the purpose of controls.

Reason: Unlabeled controls are announced as generic “button” or “link.”

Evaluation Method: Use browser accessibility inspector to check computed accessible name and role of each control.

Acceptance Criteria: Every button, link, input, and custom widget has a non-empty, meaningful accessible name. Name includes visible label text where applicable.

5. Are all images that convey information provided with equivalent text alternatives?

Purpose: Provide access to visual content for blind users.

Reason: Information in images is lost without alt text.

Evaluation Method: Inspect all <img> elements and SVG graphics. Verify that content images have descriptive alt text; decorative images have alt="" and are hidden from screen readers.

Acceptance Criteria: Content images have alt text that conveys the same information. No important text is embedded only in images.

6. Are form inputs properly labeled and associated?

Purpose: Enable users to identify and fill in form fields.

Reason: Unassociated labels cause confusion and errors.

Evaluation Method: For each input, check that clicking the label focuses the input, and that the computed accessible name is derived from the label.

Acceptance Criteria: All inputs have visible labels, and the accessible name matches or includes the visible label. Placeholder is not used as sole label.

7. Are dynamic content changes announced to screen readers without moving focus unnecessarily?

Purpose: Keep users informed of updates without disorienting them.

Reason: Blind users may miss asynchronous changes; focus moves disrupt flow.

Evaluation Method: Trigger dynamic updates (loading more items, submitting a form, receiving a notification). Observe screen reader verbosity and focus behavior.

Acceptance Criteria: Updates are announced via aria-live regions; focus stays on the user's current element unless a logical move is warranted.

8. Can users understand the page structure via headings and landmarks?

Purpose: Allow efficient navigation and page comprehension.

Reason: Screen reader users rely on headings and landmarks to skip to sections.

Evaluation Method: List all headings and landmarks with a tool. Verify hierarchy is logical and all content resides in a landmark.

Acceptance Criteria: Page has one <h1>, heading levels are not skipped; all major regions have appropriate landmarks; landmarks are uniquely labeled if multiple.

9. Is color alone never used to convey information?

Purpose: Ensure information is accessible to color-blind users.

Reason: Color-only distinctions are invisible to many users.

Evaluation Method: View the interface in grayscale (or use a color blindness simulator). Check if any meaning is lost.

Acceptance Criteria: All information communicated by color is also available through text, icons, or pattern. Links are underlined or otherwise distinct.

10. Are all motion and animations respectful of user preferences?

Purpose: Prevent vestibular harm and respect user choice.

Reason: Motion can cause seizures, nausea, distraction.

Evaluation Method: Enable prefers-reduced-motion: reduce at OS level, and also toggle in-app motion setting. Observe all transitions and animations.

Acceptance Criteria: All non-essential animations are disabled. Essential motion (like progress) is converted to non-motion indicators. No flashing >3 times/sec.

11. Can a screen reader complete the primary workflows from start to finish?

Purpose: End-to-end validation that the experience is coherent.

Reason: Isolated checks miss interaction context.

Evaluation Method: Using NVDA or VoiceOver, perform a key task (create a project, run a CLI command, chat with AI) without looking at the screen.

Acceptance Criteria: Task is completed successfully; user receives appropriate feedback at each step; no part of the flow is confusing or silent.

12. Are time limits absent or adjustable?

Purpose: Avoid rushing users who need more time.

Reason: Fixed timeouts penalize slow readers and motor-impaired users.

Evaluation Method: Identify any session timeouts, inactivity warnings, or timed interactions. Check for extend/disable options.

Acceptance Criteria: No hard timeout without warning and extension; content consumption is untimed.

13. Is content still understandable and functional when zoomed to 200%?

Purpose: Ensure low-vision users can magnify content.

Reason: Overlapping or truncated content at high zoom is unusable.

Evaluation Method: Set browser zoom to 200% (or equivalent mobile). Navigate the page; interact with forms and menus.

Acceptance Criteria: No horizontal scrolling; all text remains readable and controls are reachable; no content overlap.

14. Are error messages clear, specific, and associated with the relevant field?

Purpose: Help users recover from input errors.

Reason: Generic or distant error messages confuse and frustrate.

Evaluation Method: Submit forms with invalid data. Note error presentation, wording, and focus behavior.

Acceptance Criteria: Each error is adjacent to the field, the field is linked via aria-describedby, error text is specific (“Email is required”), and the error summary (if present) links to each field.

15. Do modal dialogs trap focus, have a proper label, and can be dismissed with Escape?

Purpose: Provide a safe, contained interaction.

Reason: Modals without focus trap and dismiss are a top accessibility bug.

Evaluation Method: Open every modal/dialog in the feature. Tab through, ensuring focus cycles within. Press Escape. Check that focus returns to trigger.

Acceptance Criteria: Focus is trapped, dialog has an accessible name, Escape and close button work, background is inert.

16. Are touch targets at least 24x24 CSS pixels with sufficient spacing?

Purpose: Enable accurate touch interaction.

Reason: Small buttons are difficult to tap.

Evaluation Method: Inspect size and padding of all interactive elements on touch devices.

Acceptance Criteria: Minimum target size of 24px; sufficient inactive space between adjacent targets.

17. Is the page language set, and are changes in language marked up?

Purpose: Ensure correct screen reader pronunciation.

Reason: Mispronounced words hinder understanding.

Evaluation Method: Check <html lang> attribute; scan for foreign phrases.

Acceptance Criteria: Language is correctly declared; any content in a different language uses lang attribute.

18. Does the content reflow correctly in a 320px viewport without loss of information or functionality?

Purpose: Support small screens and zoom.

Reason: Reflow issues hide content or require horizontal scrolling.

Evaluation Method: Resize viewport to 320px wide and interact.

Acceptance Criteria: All content visible without horizontal scroll; reading order matches logic; no overlapping.

19. Are all custom widgets compliant with ARIA design patterns and keyboard conventions?

Purpose: Ensure predictable behavior for assistive technology.

Reason: Novel interactions break mental models.

Evaluation Method: For each custom component (tabs, combobox, etc.), compare behavior against ARIA Authoring Practices Guide.

Acceptance Criteria: Roles, states, and keyboard interaction match the established pattern. Deviations are documented and justified.

20. Is the skip navigation link present and functional?

Purpose: Allow bypassing repetitive navigation.

Reason: Essential for efficiency.

Evaluation Method: On page load, press Tab. Verify skip link appears and, on activation, moves focus to main content.

Acceptance Criteria: Skip link is first focusable element, visible on focus, and correctly sets focus to main content landmark.

21. Are all iframes titled?

Purpose: Identify embedded content to screen readers.

Reason: Untitled frames are disorienting.

Evaluation Method: Inspect each iframe for a non-empty title attribute.

Acceptance Criteria: Every iframe has a descriptive title (e.g., “AI Chat Widget”).

22. Is the reading order correct when CSS is disabled?

Purpose: Ensure semantic structure stands alone.

Reason: Users with custom stylesheets or text-only browsers rely on source order.

Evaluation Method: Disable CSS and read the page.

Acceptance Criteria: Content flows in a coherent narrative; no critical content is hidden.

23. Are audio and video elements accompanied by captions, transcripts, and audio descriptions where needed?

Purpose: Provide access to multimedia.

Reason: Deaf and blind users need alternatives.

Evaluation Method: Check all media for text equivalents.

Acceptance Criteria: Pre-recorded video has captions and a transcript; video with significant visual-only content has audio description.

24. Does the terminal support screen reader navigation and announce output?

Purpose: Make CLI accessible.

Reason: Terminal is a critical interface.

Evaluation Method: With a screen reader, run commands, observe output announcement, browse output history.

Acceptance Criteria: Prompt and output are distinguishable; new output is announced; terminal is navigable with screen reader review commands.

25. Are AI chat responses announced gracefully, and is streaming manageable?

Purpose: Ensure AI interactions are accessible.

Reason: Streaming text can overwhelm screen readers.

Evaluation Method: Initiate an AI conversation; observe how streaming content is announced. Test interruption and message history navigation.

Acceptance Criteria: Response arrives in coherent chunks; live region updates do not stutter or repeat; user can interrupt with keyboard; chat history is navigable.

26. Are loading states communicated to assistive technology?

Purpose: Prevent uncertainty during waits.

Reason: A silent page seems broken.

Evaluation Method: Trigger actions with loading delays; observe screen reader output.

Acceptance Criteria: Loading indicators are announced (e.g., “Loading project list…”); progress bars have role="progressbar" and update value.

27. Does the command palette announce search results and actions?

Purpose: Make global command interface accessible.

Reason: It’s a primary navigation tool.

Evaluation Method: Open command palette, type, navigate results, execute.

Acceptance Criteria: Input has a label; number of results announced; selected item announced; execution confirmed.

28. Are tooltips accessible on hover and focus, and can they be dismissed?

Purpose: Provide supplementary info without hindrance.

Reason: Tooltips that appear only on hover are inaccessible to keyboard.

Evaluation Method: Focus the trigger element; verify tooltip appears. Press Escape; verify it dismisses.

Acceptance Criteria: Tooltip shows on both hover and focus, contains non-critical info, dismisses with Escape, and doesn’t obscure important content.

29. Is there a mechanism to report accessibility issues easily?

Purpose: Continuous feedback loop.

Reason: Users encounter issues we miss; we must be responsive.

Evaluation Method: Look for an “Accessibility feedback” link or similar in the footer or help menu.

Acceptance Criteria: Reporting channel is accessible itself; feedback is routed to the right team.

30. Does the design system enforce accessibility tokens and constraints?

Purpose: Prevent developers from inadvertently breaking accessibility.

Reason: Centralized constraints maintain consistency.

Evaluation Method: Attempt to use a low-contrast color combination defined outside the design system; observe whether tooling warns.

Acceptance Criteria: Design tokens include contrast-safe pairs; component APIs restrict inaccessible options; linters catch violations.

31. Are all interactive elements correctly marked as disabled when not actionable, and do they retain accessibility?

Purpose: Communicate unavailable controls.

Reason: Disabled buttons still need to be perceivable and their disabled state announced.

Evaluation Method: Focus a disabled button. Check if it receives focus (optional but often preferred) and that its disabled state is announced.

Acceptance Criteria: Disabled controls use disabled attribute or aria-disabled, are styled with reduced contrast but still meet minimums, and their state is correctly conveyed.

32. Are drag-and-drop interactions fully accessible with keyboard alternatives?

Purpose: Include users who cannot perform drag actions.

Reason: Drag is pointer-dependent.

Evaluation Method: Locate a draggable list or interface. Attempt to reorder or move items using only keyboard.

Acceptance Criteria: Equivalent cut/paste or move-to actions are available via keyboard or context menus; live regions announce changes.

33. Is the text spacing adaptable without breaking the layout?

Purpose: Support users who need custom spacing.

Reason: Overridden line-height, letter-spacing can cause text overflow.

Evaluation Method: Apply user stylesheet with increased letter/word/line spacing. Check for truncation or overlap.

Acceptance Criteria: All text remains fully visible and readable; no layout breakage.

34. Are high-contrast themes available and do they meet WCAG AAA contrast for body text?

Purpose: Serve users with severe low vision.

Reason: Standard contrast may be insufficient.

Evaluation Method: Switch to high-contrast theme; verify contrast ratios >= 7:1 for body text.

Acceptance Criteria: Theme is selectable, persists, and all UI elements remain distinguishable with new contrast values.

35. Do auto-updating regions (like stock tickers, live feeds) have a pause/stop mechanism?

Purpose: Allow users to control movement.

Reason: Constant motion or updates can be distracting or cause seizures.

Evaluation Method: Check for any auto-updating content that moves, blinks, or scrolls automatically. Locate pause/stop control.

Acceptance Criteria: Any animation or auto-update lasting >5 seconds can be paused, stopped, or hidden by the user.

36. Are all session timeout warnings announced with sufficient time to extend?

Purpose: Prevent data loss.

Reason: Users need enough warning to react.

Evaluation Method: Simulate session timeout; observe timing of warning and whether it’s accessible.

Acceptance Criteria: Warning appears at least 2 minutes before timeout, is announced via alert, and provides a simple keyboard-accessible “Extend” button.

37. Are data visualizations accompanied by accessible data tables or text summaries?

Purpose: Make complex data perceivable.

Reason: Charts are visual-only by default.

Evaluation Method: For each chart/graph, find the alternative text or data table link.

Acceptance Criteria: A “View data table” option or equivalent textual summary is available; chart has a meaningful aria-label.

38. Is the AI’s reasoning and tool execution available in an accessible, expandable format?

Purpose: Provide transparency without clutter.

Reason: Some users need to inspect AI process; others don’t.

Evaluation Method: Trigger a complex AI task. Look for “Show reasoning” toggle. Expand and check screen reader access.

Acceptance Criteria: Reasoning is in a disclosure with aria-expanded; content has proper headings; tool calls are labeled.

39. Are all keyboard shortcuts remappable and documented?

Purpose: Avoid conflicts and support user preference.

Reason: Screen readers use modifier keys; hardcoded shortcuts break.

Evaluation Method: Open keyboard shortcuts help. Check for conflict warnings. Attempt to remap a shortcut.

Acceptance Criteria: A shortcuts dialog is accessible; remapping is possible for global shortcuts; conflicts are detected and flagged.

40. Does the interface avoid flashing content?

Purpose: Prevent seizures.

Reason: Flashing >3 Hz is dangerous.

Evaluation Method: Use a photosensitive epilepsy analysis tool (e.g., PEAT) on any animated component.

Acceptance Criteria: No component flashes more than 3 times per second in any state.

41. Are cookie consent and other obstructive dialogs fully keyboard accessible and do not trap focus without escape?

Purpose: Ensure legal and critical popups don’t block access.

Reason: Many consent dialogs are notorious accessibility failures.

Evaluation Method: Encounter cookie banner; navigate with Tab; attempt to accept or customize.

Acceptance Criteria: Dialog is in the tab order, can be operated, and dismissed with Escape or a button. Does not permanently trap focus.

42. Is the print version of the page usable and does it include URLs for links?

Purpose: Support users who print content.

Reason: Some users need hard copies for reading.

Evaluation Method: Print preview a page; check for missing content, broken layout, and link URLs.

Acceptance Criteria: All main content is present; links show URLs or footnotes; no critical information lost.

43. Are alternative text for complex images (diagrams, infographics) sufficiently detailed?

Purpose: Provide equivalent information.

Reason: A short alt text may not suffice.

Evaluation Method: Identify any complex image; check that a long description (e.g., aria-describedby pointing to a detailed text) is provided.

Acceptance Criteria: Complex images have a long description that explains the content and structure.

44. Do live regions not cause excessive announcements?

Purpose: Prevent screen reader noise.

Reason: Too many announcements are overwhelming.

Evaluation Method: With screen reader running, use a feature with dynamic updates (chat, live feed). Listen for redundant announcements.

Acceptance Criteria: Announcements are meaningful, not repeated rapidly, and do not interrupt current speech unnecessarily unless critical.

45. Are form multi-step wizards accessible, with step indicators and easy navigation?

Purpose: Support complex input flows.

Reason: Wizards can confuse if progress is not clear.

Evaluation Method: Navigate a multi-step form. Move forward and backward; check announcements.

Acceptance Criteria: Current step is indicated (e.g., “Step 2 of 4: Address”); Back and Next buttons are labeled; form data is preserved.

46. Does the UI handle 400% zoom without loss of content or functionality?

Purpose: Accommodate extreme zoom.

Reason: Some users need very large text; reflow is essential.

Evaluation Method: Zoom to 400% on desktop viewport. Navigate and interact.

Acceptance Criteria: Content reflows to a single column; all functionality available; no horizontal scrolling except for intrinsically wide elements like data tables (which then must have alternative means).

47. Are there sufficient ways to contact support that are accessible (e.g., not phone-only)?

Purpose: Provide help access to all.

Reason: Deaf users can’t use voice-only phone; some cannot type quickly in live chat.

Evaluation Method: Check support options: email, web form, chat.

Acceptance Criteria: Multiple accessible contact methods; any real-time chat must be accessible.

48. Is the user’s accessibility preferences applied before any content is visible?

Purpose: Avoid flash of inaccessible content.

Reason: A momentary un-themed or animated flash can cause pain.

Evaluation Method: Set reduced motion, high contrast, and larger text in OS. Load Zenuxs; observe initial render.

Acceptance Criteria: The first paint respects motion and theme preferences; no FART (Flash of Animations, Repulsive Theme).

49. Are there any keyboard traps in custom widgets (dropdowns, autocompletes, modals)?

Purpose: Ensure users can escape.

Reason: Trap is a showstopper.

Evaluation Method: Tab through every interactive component; try to exit using standard keys.

Acceptance Criteria: Escape or Tab+Shift consistently exits; no element permanently captures focus.

50. Are all aria attributes used correctly and not overridden on native elements?

Purpose: Avoid breaking native semantics.

Reason: Misused ARIA is worse than no ARIA.

Evaluation Method: Lint codebase for ARIA misuse (e.g., aria-label on a <div> without role, aria-hidden on focusable element, etc.).

Acceptance Criteria: Zero ARIA errors in automated linting; manual review confirms usage follows spec.

51. Is the terminal’s color scheme high-contrast and customizable?

Purpose: Visual accessibility in terminal.

Reason: Many default terminal schemes have poor contrast.

Evaluation Method: Check contrast of default terminal theme; verify ability to switch themes.

Acceptance Criteria: Default theme meets WCAG AA contrast; multiple high-contrast themes available.

52. Are links visually distinguishable from surrounding text, even without color?

Purpose: Identify interactive text.

Reason: Underlining or other non-color indicator is needed.

Evaluation Method: View page in grayscale; check if links are still identifiable.

Acceptance Criteria: Links are underlined or have a distinct non-color style (e.g., bold, border-bottom).

53. Can the AI explain its own accessibility?

Purpose: Self-documenting accessibility.

Reason: Users may ask AI how to access a feature; AI must give accurate accessibility guidance.

Evaluation Method: Ask the AI: “How do I use the command palette with a screen reader?” Verify answer.

Acceptance Criteria: AI provides correct instructions that reflect actual accessible interaction.

54. Are all third-party embeds (Twitter, YouTube, etc.) assessed for accessibility, and fallbacks provided?

Purpose: Ensure external content doesn't break experience.

Reason: Embeds are often inaccessible.

Evaluation Method: Identify embeds; test with keyboard and screen reader. If inaccessible, find alternative presentation.

Acceptance Criteria: Inaccessible embeds are wrapped or replaced with static, accessible equivalents; an accessible link to external content is provided.

55. Does the error summary at the top of a form link to the specific fields?

Purpose: Quick error navigation.

Reason: Users with screen readers can jump to errors.

Evaluation Method: Submit a form with multiple errors; check the summary list.

Acceptance Criteria: Summary contains links that, when activated, move focus to the corresponding field.

56. Are all icons that convey meaning provided with a text alternative?

Purpose: Convey icon semantics.

Reason: An icon without text is meaningless to screen readers.

Evaluation Method: Inspect icon-only buttons, status icons, etc.

Acceptance Criteria: Icons have aria-label or equivalent accessible name; decorative icons have aria-hidden="true".

57. Is the document title descriptive and updated on navigation?

Purpose: Orient users.

Reason: Screen reader announces page title on load.

Evaluation Method: Navigate between pages; observe document title.

Acceptance Criteria: Title is unique, describes page content (e.g., “Project Dashboard – Zenuxs”), and updates on client-side navigation.

58. Are CAPTCHA challenges avoided or provided with accessible alternatives?

Purpose: Not exclude users.

Reason: CAPTCHA is a known barrier.

Evaluation Method: Check sign-up or contact forms for CAPTCHA.

Acceptance Criteria: No traditional CAPTCHA; if present, an accessible fallback (e.g., audio + logic) exists and works.

59. Can a user navigate the entire site using voice control?

Purpose: Support voice navigation.

Reason: Growing user base.

Evaluation Method: Use Dragon NaturallySpeaking or Voice Control (macOS/iOS) to speak commands like “Click Projects.”

Acceptance Criteria: All interactive elements have visible labels that can be spoken; voice commands activate the correct element.

60. Is there a consistent mechanism to skip repetitive blocks?

Purpose: Efficiency.

Reason: Screen reader users waste time on repeated navigation.

Evaluation Method: Check for skip links and landmark structure.

Acceptance Criteria: Skip links and landmarks enable bypassing blocks in all pages.

61. Are the AI’s streaming responses throttled to avoid screen reader overload?

Purpose: Manage announcements.

Reason: Every token announcement is disruptive.

Evaluation Method: Use AI chat with screen reader; observe update frequency.

Acceptance Criteria: Live region updates occur at sentence boundaries or after debounce; no more than 2 updates per second.

62. Does the terminal support copy/paste via keyboard and screen reader accessible buffers?

Purpose: Standard interaction.

Reason: Blind users need to copy output.

Evaluation Method: Run command, select output with screen reader, copy, paste elsewhere.

Acceptance Criteria: Terminal output is accessible as text; standard copy commands work.

63. Are there any interactions that depend solely on device motion?

Purpose: Avoid motion-only features.

Reason: Inaccessible to many.

Evaluation Method: Identify any shake/tilt-based features.

Acceptance Criteria: Such features have static UI alternatives and can be disabled.

64. Are all interactive components fully operable in high contrast mode on Windows?

Purpose: Support Windows High Contrast Mode.

Reason: This mode alters color mapping; many sites break.

Evaluation Method: Enable Windows High Contrast Black theme; use Zenuxs.

Acceptance Criteria: All text readable; controls visible; focus indicators distinct.

65. Is the language of parts correctly marked for multilingual content?

Purpose: Proper pronunciation.

Reason: Multilingual pages without lang are read incorrectly.

Evaluation Method: Locate any non-English phrases; check for lang attribute.

Acceptance Criteria: Any phrase in a different language is wrapped in a <span lang="..."> or equivalent.
